proposals.title: Spectral Reconstruction of the Universal Space: From Arithmetic Invariants to the Classifying C*-Algebra
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the classifying space of a C*-algebra or a group is a fundamental tool, often used to compute K-theory or classify foliations. However, in deep arithmetic problems like the Riemann hypothesis or class field theory, the natural 'space' is not given a priori but must be reconstructed from spectral data (e.g., zeros of zeta functions, Frobenius eigenvalues). This inversion of perspective—treating the universal space as the unknown—could unify spectral triples with arithmetic topology.
proposals.hypothesis: The spectral data (poles/zeros of L-functions, eigenvalues of Frobenius) associated to a number ring or a knot complement can be realized as the spectrum of a Dirac operator on a yet-to-be-constructed universal geometric object, whose associated C*-algebra and spectral triple classify the arithmetic structure.
proposals.method: We will employ Connes's framework of 'zeta spectral triples,' where the zeta function of a Dirac operator is given. The core technical task is to solve the inverse spectral problem: given a suitable zeta function (e.g., the completed Riemann zeta function or the local factors from recent q-series work), reconstruct the Dirac operator and its associated spectral triple. This involves adapting the 'moment problem' techniques from recent pre-2026 papers to the noncommutative measure theory of states on the putative universal C*-algebra. The reconstruction will use the cyclic homology of the 'ring' of noncommutative differential forms as a constraint on the possible classifying space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new spectral reconstruction principle for noncommutative geometry, directly linking arithmetic invariants to operator algebras. It aims to produce a concrete, universal C*-algebraic model for the 'space' underlying the Riemann zeta zeros, offering a novel geometric perspective on Hilbert's 8th problem. Furthermore, it will extend the dictionary between knots and primes by constructing a common classifying framework from spectral data.
proposals.novelty: While spectral triples are built on a given algebra, this proposal inverts the process, making the universal classifying space the output of an arithmetic spectral problem. This moves beyond using noncommutative geometry as a tool for analysis, to treating it as the fundamental object to be deduced from number theory.

proposals.title: Cohomological Formulation of the Riemann Hypothesis via Spectral Triples and Zeta Cycles
proposals.motivation: The Riemann Hypothesis (RH) has deep connections to spectral theory and noncommutative geometry, notably through Connes' spectral realization of zeros and the construction of 'zeta spectral triples'. However, these approaches are fundamentally homological, constructing a trace whose singularities correspond to zeta zeros. A cohomological dual could reveal new structural invariants and provide a more robust topological framework for RH, analogous to how cohomology theories often offer finer classification tools than homology alone in algebraic geometry and topology.
proposals.hypothesis: That the Riemann Hypothesis can be equivalently formulated as a statement about the non-triviality of a specific cohomology class (a 'zeta cocycle') constructed from the data of a spectral triple associated to the ring of adeles of the rationals. This cohomology should arise from a cyclic cohomology theory dual to the homology theory giving the spectral trace.
proposals.method: We will construct the dual theory by applying the cohomological version of the Connes-Moscovici local index formula to the 'zeta spectral triple'. This involves: 1) Defining a cyclic cocycle from the spectral data and the Dirac operator of the triple, using the framework of twisted spectral triples if necessary to handle the scaling action. 2) Interpreting the pairing of this cocycle with K-theory classes as generating a cohomological 'L-function'. 3) Using the 'q-series' and moment problem techniques from Connes' recent work to analyze the analytic properties of this cohomological invariant and relate its poles/zeros to those of the Riemann zeta function.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel cohomological framework for the Riemann Hypothesis, potentially leading to new invariants in noncommutative geometry and number theory. It may also forge a stronger link between the operator-theoretic methods of noncommutative geometry and the cohomological techniques of arithmetic geometry, such as those in class field theory and the theory of motives.
proposals.novelty: While spectral triples and cyclic homology are standard tools in noncommutative geometry, their systematic application to produce a cohomological dual to the spectral realization of zeta zeros is unexplored. The novelty lies in explicitly constructing and analyzing the 'zeta cocycle' and its associated cohomology theory as a direct probe for the Riemann Hypothesis.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Spectral Geometries from K-Theoretic Data: An Inverse Problem for the Riemann Zeta Zeros
proposals.motivation: The deep conjectural link between the zeros of the Riemann zeta function and the spectrum of a hypothetical 'zeta operator' suggests an underlying geometric space. Pre-2026 work, including Connes's spectral triples and the 'Zeta Spectral Triples' framework, provides a map from geometry to K-theory. The inverse problem—deducing the geometry from its K-theoretic invariants—remains a profound challenge with implications for noncommutative geometry and number theory.
proposals.hypothesis: The K-theory data (specifically the K-homology class) of the conjectural spectral triple associated to the Riemann zeta function contains sufficient information to reconstruct key geometric and spectral properties of the underlying noncommutative space, including constraints on the distribution of zeta zeros.
proposals.method: We will employ the framework of 'Zeta Spectral Triples' and the associated C*-algebras. The core method involves analyzing the index map in K-homology and its pairing with cyclic cohomology, as defined by the Chern-Connes character. Specifically, we will study the inverse problem of determining the spectral triple's metric dimension and Dixmier trace from the image of its K-homology class under this character. This will be tested within the explicit 'q-series' and local factors frameworks from recent work, using techniques from the moment problem to constrain the spectral measure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous K-theoretic formulation of the inverse spectral problem for the Riemann zeta zeros, advancing the noncommutative geometric approach to the Riemann Hypothesis. It will establish new constraints on possible 'zeta geometries' and potentially reveal new invariants distinguishing candidate spectral triples.
proposals.novelty: While the forward construction of K-theory from a spectral triple is standard, the systematic inversion of this process for number-theoretic spectral triples is novel. It directly addresses the recovery of geometric data from algebraic topology, a central but under-explored question in the field.

proposals.title: Spectral Geometries from Non-Commutative Dynamics: Reversing the Role of the Commutative Subalgebra
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the spectral triple framework traditionally begins with a commutative C*-subalgebra, whose spectrum defines the underlying 'space'. This approach, while powerful, inherently privileges commutative structures and may limit the exploration of genuinely noncommutative geometric phenomena. A reversal of this hierarchy could reveal new classes of quantum spaces and provide a fresh perspective on the spectral realization of zeta zeros and other arithmetic invariants.
proposals.hypothesis: That a spectral geometry can be constructed axiomatically from a chosen noncommutative dynamical system or operator algebraic data, with the classical commutative structure emerging as a derived invariant rather than a foundational input.
proposals.method: The approach will construct spectral triples where the Dirac operator and its spectrum are primary. We will start from specific noncommutative algebras appearing in Connes's recent work, such as those related to q-series, the moment problem, or knot theory. The goal is to axiomatically define a 'spectral state' or a dynamics on the algebra, from which a canonical maximal abelian subalgebra (if it exists) is extracted via a spectral flow or a stability condition under the modular group. This will be tested within the framework of 'zeta spectral triples' to see if the Riemann zeros can dictate the emergence of a commutative structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will expand the axiomatic foundation of noncommutative geometry, offering a new method to generate and classify spectral triples. It aims to produce novel examples of 'non-commutative-led' geometries with potential applications to understanding the spectral nature of arithmetic, possibly leading to a dynamical origin for the Frobenius in class field theory.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the foundational logical flow of noncommutative geometry, making the noncommutative dynamics geometrically primitive. This moves beyond using noncommutativity to study classical spaces, towards defining spaces by their noncommutative dynamics.

proposals.title: Non-Abelian Scaling Actions and Spectral Triples: Towards a Group-Theoretic Refinement of the Riemann Zeros
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the scaling action by the abelian group R+* is fundamental to the theory of spectral triples and the reconstruction of metric dimension. This structure underpins the spectral interpretation of the Riemann zeta zeros via the Connes-Marcolli Bost-Connes system and related q-series constructions. However, this abelian framework may be too rigid to capture more intricate arithmetic symmetries or the hypothetical noncommutative geometry underlying the full Langlands program, limiting the spectral realization of L-functions associated to non-abelian Galois representations.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the abelian scaling group with a non-abelian group (e.g., a semidirect product, a matrix group, or a finite non-abelian group) in the construction of spectral triples will generate new classes of noncommutative spaces whose associated spectral zeta functions encode more sophisticated arithmetic data, potentially related to zeros of non-abelian L-functions.
proposals.method: The research will proceed by constructing explicit examples within the established framework of spectral triples and crossed product C*-algebras. First, we will replace the R+* action in a known system (e.g., a modification of the Bost-Connes system or the `zeta spectral triple' model) with an action of a carefully chosen non-abelian group G. The core technical work will involve computing the associated spectral zeta function and its poles via the Connes-Moscovici local index formula adapted to the G-action. We will analyze the high-energy asymptotics of the Dirac operator to define a generalized notion of dimension and study the induced time evolution on the associated quantum statistical mechanical system, seeking a phase transition with spontaneous symmetry breaking.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the toolkit of noncommutative geometry by introducing a new class of geometries based on non-abelian scaling. It aims to produce a novel spectral model where the dimension spectrum of a space is not a simple number but carries a group-theoretic signature, potentially offering a new spectral pathway to the zeros of automorphic L-functions and enriching the dialogue between noncommutative geometry and class field theory.
proposals.novelty: While the role of group actions is central to noncommutative geometry, the scaling action is almost universally taken to be abelian. Systematically employing a non-abelian group for this foundational structure is a radical departure from existing work, moving beyond the thermodynamics of abelian groups to explore the `non-abelian thermodynamics' of spectral data.

proposals.title: Spectral Action on the Adeles Class Space and the Explicit Formula of Number Theory
proposals.motivation: The adeles class space, a cornerstone of Connes' noncommutative geometry approach to the Riemann hypothesis, provides a spectral interpretation of the zeros of the Riemann zeta function. The spectral action principle, a fundamental tool in noncommutative geometry that yields gravity from pure spectral data, has been successfully applied to physical models but not yet fully exploited in this arithmetic context. Computing it directly on this space could forge a deeper, dynamical link between the operator-theoretic framework and the deep analytic invariants of number theory.
proposals.hypothesis: The bosonic spectral action Tr(f(D/Λ)) for the natural spectral triple of the adeles class space, when computed and regularized, will produce a geometric interpretation of the Weil explicit formula, thereby encoding the Riemann zeta zeros as a spectral output of a generalized Dirac operator.
proposals.method: We will construct the explicit spectral triple (A, H, D) for the adeles class space, building on Connes' prior work. The core technical step is the computation of the spectral action trace Tr(f(D/Λ)) using the Poisson summation formula on the adeles and the theory of Sobolev spaces. This involves a careful analysis of the spectrum of the scaling operator D, separating contributions from the idele class group and the cokernel of the inclusion map. The regularization will employ the zeta regularization techniques Connes has developed for 'zeta spectral triples', aiming to isolate the number-theoretic terms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a dynamical, action-based derivation of the explicit formula from first principles of noncommutative geometry, strengthening the spectral interpretation of the zeta zeros. It will also expand the applicability of the spectral action principle beyond high-energy physics, demonstrating its power as a universal computational tool in arithmetic geometry.
proposals.novelty: While the link between the adeles class space and the explicit formula is known, deriving it directly from the spectral action—a physical variational principle—is a novel synthesis of Connes' recent work on zeta spectral triples with his foundational arithmetic framework.

proposals.title: Spectral Reconstruction of Noncommutative Spaces: An Inverse Problem for Spectral Triples
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, a spectral triple (A, H, D) encodes a 'space' via the spectrum of a Dirac operator D. The forward problem—computing spectral invariants like the zeta function from a given triple—is well-studied, particularly for understanding the Riemann zeros. The inverse problem, reconstructing the algebra A and its geometric structure from spectral data alone, remains a profound and largely open challenge. Solving it would establish a deeper, more intrinsic link between spectral theory and geometry, akin to hearing the shape of a drum but in a quantum context.
proposals.hypothesis: Given a suitable set of spectral data (e.g., the spectrum of D, residues of its zeta function, and signs of its eigenvalues), one can reconstruct, up to unitary equivalence, the associated noncommutative space as a spectral triple, including key aspects of its coordinate algebra and Hochschild cycle.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage Connes' reconstruction theorem for commutative geometries, which provides an axiomatic characterization of spectral triples of Riemannian spin manifolds. We will first formulate necessary and sufficient spectral conditions for a 'Dirac spectrum' to arise from a commutative spectral triple. For the noncommutative case, we will develop an algorithmic framework using the 'spectral realization' of zeros of zeta functions and the theory of quantum statistical mechanical systems. This involves analyzing the moment problem associated to local factors (as in Connes' recent work) to recover algebraic data from spectral measures, and employing tools from the classification of factors in von Neumann algebras to constrain the possible reconstructed algebras.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous mathematical framework for the spectral inverse problem in noncommutative geometry, potentially leading to new spectral characterizations of singular quantum spaces. It will also offer a novel perspective on the spectral interpretation of the Riemann zeros, by clarifying what geometric data they might encode if they arise as an absorption spectrum.
proposals.novelty: While the forward spectral problem is central to the field, a systematic program to invert it—moving from a quantum mechanical spectrum back to a pre-geometric algebraic structure—is a fundamentally new direction. It transcends existing work on spectral actions and zeta invariants by asking for a complete reconstruction of the underlying noncommutative space.

proposals.title: The Scaling Orbit Space and its Spectral Dual: A Noncommutative Geometric Approach to Zeta Zeros
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the adèle class space, a quotient by the scaling action of the positive reals, is central to the spectral realization of Riemann zeta zeros. However, quotienting often discards information about the dynamics of the scaling group itself. Studying the dual space of scaling orbits, rather than the quotient, could reveal new spectral data and provide a more intrinsic geometric understanding of the zeros.
proposals.hypothesis: The space of scaling orbits, when analyzed through its associated noncommutative algebra and spectral triple, encodes refined spectral information, potentially isolating contributions from specific families of zeta zeros or revealing new invariants related to the moment problem for local L-factors.
proposals.method: We will construct a `spectral triple for the orbit space` by inverting the quotient procedure. This involves defining a C*-algebra of functions invariant along orbits but with controlled transverse behavior, and a Dirac operator incorporating the generator of the scaling flow. The spectral action principle and the associated zeta functions will be computed. This framework will be tested on model systems, including those related to the Bost-Connes system and the q-series from recent work, using techniques from cyclic cohomology and the moment problem to analyze the spectral measure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new geometric object—the orbit space dual—for analyzing number-theoretic spectra. It aims to refine the spectral correspondence for zeta zeros and potentially offer a new pathway to understand the horizontal distribution of zeros via the scaling flow's spectral decomposition.
proposals.novelty: While the quotient space is well-studied, systematically constructing its 'inverse'—the space of orbits as a noncommutative space in its own right—is a novel dual perspective. It applies Connes's recent work on `Zeta Spectral Triples` and the moment problem in a new, more symmetric geometric context.

proposals.title: Spectral Zeta-Invariants and Particle Generations from Noncommutative Finite Algebras
proposals.motivation: The noncommutative geometric description of the Standard Model, based on the finite spectral triple of the algebra A_F = C ⊕ H ⊕ M_3(C), successfully encodes the known particle content and Higgs mechanism. However, it offers no fundamental geometric principle for predicting the number of fermion generations (three) or for accommodating potential new particles. This work seeks to address this limitation by exploring a broader landscape of finite spectral data.
proposals.hypothesis: That a systematic classification and deformation of the finite-dimensional algebra and its spectral triple, guided by constraints from the spectral action principle and zeta-function regularization, can generate a discrete set of viable models, potentially explaining the three-generation structure and predicting new, heavy scalar or vector bosons.
proposals.method: We will employ Connes' framework of spectral triples and the associated spectral zeta functions. The core approach is to parameterize finite, real spectral triples (A_F, H_F, D_F, J_F, γ_F) beyond the Standard Model case, imposing the axioms of noncommutative geometry. We will compute the spectral action Tr(f(D/Λ)) for these candidates, expanding it via heat kernel/zeta regularization to obtain the particle Lagrangian. The classification will be constrained by demanding anomaly cancellation, Poincaré duality in K-theory, and the existence of a non-degenerate fermion mass matrix. Computational algebra packages (e.g., in SageMath) will be used to explore matrix algebras and their representations.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a classified landscape of finite spectral geometries compatible with the spectral action principle. It will establish necessary algebraic conditions for the emergence of three fermion generations and may identify minimal extensions of A_F whose spectral action yields new physics signatures, such as a Z' boson or scalar dark matter candidates, thereby providing testable predictions from geometric first principles.
proposals.novelty: While the Standard Model algebra A_F is well-studied, a systematic, zeta-function-driven exploration of its deformations and alternatives within the spectral triple formalism, with the explicit goal of deriving particle generations, has not been comprehensively undertaken. The novelty lies in applying the 'zeta spectral triple' formalism directly to this model-building problem.

proposals.title: Gravitational Duals of Spectral Actions: A Geometric Interpretation of the Full Spectral Action with Fermions
proposals.motivation: The spectral action principle, a cornerstone of noncommutative geometry (NCG), successfully derives the Standard Model coupled to gravity from a purely spectral datum. However, its formulation remains algebraic and lacks a manifestly geometric or gravitational dual description in the spirit of gauge/gravity duality. Finding such a dual would deepen our understanding of the gravitational sector emergent from NCG and potentially reveal new connections between number theory, quantum fields, and geometry.
proposals.hypothesis: The full spectral action for an almost-commutative geometry, including its fermionic sector, can be reformulated as the effective action of a specific higher-spin or generalized gravitational theory on a dual geometric space, where the Dirac operator's spectrum is encoded in the geometry's curvature invariants.
proposals.method: We will employ Connes' framework of spectral triples and zeta regularization. First, we will analyze the heat kernel expansion of the full spectral action, isolating terms from the gauge and fermionic parts. Using the 'Zeta Spectral Triples' formalism, we will interpret these terms as arising from a spectral zeta function of a dual geometric operator. The core technical step is to construct a classical gravitational Lagrangian, whose variation yields equations linking its curvature to the parameters of the finite noncommutative space (the 'class' in class field theory). This will involve solving an inverse spectral problem, guided by the moment problem techniques from Connes' recent work on local factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first explicit gravitational theory dual to the complete particle physics content of the NCG Standard Model. It will establish a new dictionary between algebraic spectral data and classical geometric objects, potentially offering a novel geometric explanation for the Higgs mechanism and fermion masses. The results could also suggest new avenues for incorporating quantum gravitational corrections within the NCG framework.
proposals.novelty: While the bosonic part of the spectral action resembles gravity, the proposed duality explicitly incorporates the fermionic sector, which has no direct classical geometric analogue. The novelty lies in using the zeta function of the entire Dirac operator of the almost-commutative geometry to define a dual geometric space, moving beyond mere analogy to a precise mathematical correspondence.

proposals.title: Spectral Zeta Invariants and the Non-Commutative Length Element: A Framework Beyond Tomita's Involution
proposals.motivation: In non-commutative geometry, the spectral triple framework encodes geometric data via a Dirac operator D. The non-commutative length element ds = D^{-1} is fundamental, but its rigorous definition in the non-unital setting often relies on Tomita's modular involution J from the theory of von Neumann algebras. This reliance restricts the framework's applicability to algebraic structures not naturally equipped with a Tomita theory, such as certain q-deformed or number-theoretic spectral models. A formulation independent of J is needed to broaden the scope of non-commutative metric geometry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the zeta-regularized residue of the Dirac operator's modulus, |D|^{-s}, evaluated via spectral zeta functions, provides a canonical and purely spectral-theoretic definition of the length element ds, circumventing the need for Tomita's involution J.
proposals.method: We will construct spectral triples (A, H, D) for target spaces (e.g., certain function rings from q-series or adelic contexts) where a natural Tomita operator is absent. The core method is to define ds via the singular trace given by the Dixmier trace, but deriving its properties directly from the meromorphic continuation of the spectral zeta function ζ_D(s) = Tr(|D|^{-s}). We will analyze the first residue of ζ_D(s) at its pole, which defines a weight, and prove its compatibility with the non-commutative integral. This approach leverages Connes' spectral characterization of the Dixmier trace and the moment problem associated to local factors to ensure finiteness and positivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a more flexible definition of the non-commutative infinitesimal, applicable to spectral triples arising in number theory (e.g., from class field theory) and quantum groups. It will provide a new pathway to define metric structures in cases where the modular theory is not available, thereby extending the toolkit of non-commutative geometry. The results will be tested explicitly on the spectral models hinted at in recent pre-2026 work on q-series and local factors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in decoupling the foundational definition of ds from the modular operator framework, using instead the intrinsic spectral zeta residue as the primary invariant. This shift in perspective is non-obvious because Tomita's theory has been deeply embedded in the definition of the non-commutative integral since its inception.

proposals.title: Spectral Classification of Unitary Representations in Noncommutative Algebraic Structures
proposals.motivation: The classification of unitary representations is foundational to quantum physics and noncommutative geometry, yet a systematic framework for general algebras remains elusive. Pre-2026 work, such as Connes' spectral triples for the Standard Model, demonstrates the physical power of specific representations but does not provide a general theory. A unified classification scheme would bridge operator algebras, representation theory, and number theory, offering new tools for exploring quantum symmetries and spectral models of zeta functions.
proposals.hypothesis: That the unitary representations of a broad class of non-commutative algebras can be classified by invariants derived from the spectral data of associated Dirac operators and zeta functions, extending the paradigm of spectral triples.
proposals.method: The approach will construct a new framework by integrating Connes' recent methods: (1) Employ 'Zeta Spectral Triples' to associate a canonical spectral zeta function to a representation. (2) Use the 'moment problem associated to local factors' to analyze the spectral measure and classify its type. (3) Apply techniques from 'Knots, primes and class field theory' to explore the role of symmetry groups and Galois actions in the representation space. The classification will proceed by analyzing the singularities and residues of these spectral zeta functions as complete invariants.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a systematic taxonomy of unitary representations for algebras beyond the Standard Model, providing a new 'spectral representation theory'. It will deliver concrete criteria for the existence and uniqueness of representations fitting given spectral data, thereby creating a powerful new language for constructing physical models in noncommutative geometry.
proposals.novelty: While spectral triples encode geometric data, using the analytic properties of their associated zeta functions as the primary tool for classifying all unitary representations of an algebra is a novel inversion of the standard perspective. This moves from constructing a specific triple to classifying all possible realizations of an algebra on Hilbert space via spectral invariants.

proposals.title: Persistent Homology of Spectral Triples: A Topological Analysis of Noncommutative Geometries
proposals.motivation: Spectral triples, the central objects in Alain Connes' noncommutative geometry, encode geometric and metric data of potentially singular spaces through spectral properties of a Dirac operator. However, extracting and classifying the nuanced topological and geometric information from the spectrum of these operators, especially for exotic or arithmetic examples, remains a profound challenge. Persistent homology, a robust tool from topological data analysis (TDA) for quantifying multiscale topological features in complex data, offers a novel, pre-2026 computational framework to analyze this spectral data systematically.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the persistent homology barcodes derived from the filtered simplicial complexes built from the spectral data (e.g., eigenvalues, eigenvectors, or associated heat kernels) of a spectral triple provide stable, computable invariants that capture essential topological and geometric information of the underlying noncommutative space, complementing traditional spectral invariants like the zeta function.
proposals.method: The method involves constructing a point cloud or a filtered simplicial complex from the spectral data of a given spectral triple (A, H, D). For a finite-dimensional approximation, we will use the first N eigenvalues and corresponding eigenvectors of |D| to define a metric space. We will then apply the Vietoris-Rips or witness complex construction to this space and compute its persistent homology using standard algorithms (e.g., as implemented in the GUDHI or Perseus libraries). For infinite-dimensional cases, we will study finite-rank approximations from the spectral action or the associated heat kernel exp(-tD^2), using the parameter t to define the filtration. The resulting barcodes will be compared across families of spectral triples (e.g., for different parameters q in q-deformed geometries or for the 'zeta spectral triples' associated to number theory).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new set of computable, stable topological invariants for noncommutative geometries, potentially offering a finer tool for classification than the spectral dimension or zeta residues alone. It will establish a concrete bridge between Connes' framework and applied topology, opening the possibility of using TDA to probe the structure of quantum and arithmetic spaces where classical topology fails.
proposals.novelty: While persistent homology is widely applied to point clouds from empirical data, its application to the abstract, operator-algebraic data of spectral triples is entirely novel. The novelty lies in translating the spectral and metric data of a Dirac operator into a form amenable to TDA, creating a new dialogue between pure noncommutative geometry and computational topology.

proposals.title: Finite-Dimensional Spectral Approximations and the Zeta-Regularized Determinant
proposals.motivation: Spectral triples provide a noncommutative geometric framework for Riemannian spin manifolds and singular quantum spaces. A central challenge is the computational and conceptual translation of infinite-dimensional spectral data into finite, tractable forms, particularly for applications in number theory where the spectral action and zeta-regularized determinants are paramount. Discrete approximations could bridge Connes' work on zeta spectral triples and the explicit computation of invariants like the Connes-Moscovici local index formula.
proposals.hypothesis: That for a suitable class of spectral triples (A, H, D), one can construct sequences of finite-dimensional matrix algebras A_n and operators D_n whose spectral zeta functions and regularized determinants converge to those of the original triple, preserving key invariants.
proposals.method: We will employ the theory of spectral truncations and fuzzy geometries, adapting methods from noncommutative geometry and matrix models. For a spectral triple, we will project onto the eigenspaces of |D| below a cutoff Λ to obtain finite matrix data (A_Λ, D_Λ). The core technical step will be to analyze the zeta function ζ_{D_Λ}(s) = Tr(|D_Λ|^{-s}) and its meromorphic continuation, comparing it to ζ_D(s) via perturbation theory. We will specifically test this framework on the Riemann zeta zeros spectral triple and on Connes' q-series examples, implementing numerical checks of determinant convergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous discretization procedure for spectral triples, enabling numerical exploration of spectral actions and index theorems. It will establish a concrete bridge between the abstract formalism of noncommutative geometry and finite-dimensional matrix models, potentially offering new insights into the spectral interpretation of zeta zeros.
proposals.novelty: While spectral truncations exist, a systematic study of the convergence of the full zeta-regularized determinant—a global spectral invariant—under such approximations is unexplored. Applying this to the number-theoretic spectral triples central to Connes' recent work is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Path Integral Quantization of the Spectral Action: A Noncommutative Geometric Approach to Quantum Gravity and the Standard Model
proposals.motivation: The spectral action principle, derived from noncommutative geometry, provides a compelling unified description of the Standard Model coupled to gravity as a purely gravitational theory on a specific product geometry. However, its quantization remains a foundational challenge. Developing a rigorous quantum field theory framework based on the spectral action is essential to test its physical predictions, such as the nature of the Higgs potential, unification scales, and potential quantum gravitational corrections, moving it from a classical action to a full quantum theory.
proposals.hypothesis: The spectral action can serve as the classical action in a well-defined path integral quantization, leading to a consistent quantum theory whose perturbative expansion yields the quantized Standard Model coupled to Einstein gravity, plus specific higher-order corrections and a predictive UV completion rooted in the spectral data of a Dirac operator.
proposals.method: We will employ the framework of spectral triples and zeta regularization. The core method involves defining the generating functional Z = ∫ exp(-S_spectral[D]) d[D] via a path integral over fluctuations of the Dirac operator D within the space of admissible spectral triples. The measure d[D] will be constructed using the spectral zeta function for the Laplacian on this space. Perturbative expansion will be performed by splitting D = D_0 + A, where D_0 is the classical background (product of the canonical triple for a 4-manifold and a finite matrix geometry), and A contains the quantum gauge, Higgs, and gravitational fluctuations. Feynman rules will be derived from the heat kernel expansion of Tr(f(D/Λ)). Renormalization will be analyzed using the noncommutative geometry version of the Wilsonian renormalization group flow for the spectral action.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic path integral quantization procedure for the full spectral action, establishing a concrete bridge between noncommutative geometry and quantum field theory. It will yield explicit computational techniques for quantum corrections within the model, allowing for testable predictions beyond the classical approximation and potentially revealing a novel mechanism for UV/IR mixing or regularization inherent to the spectral formalism.
proposals.novelty: While the spectral action is well-studied classically, its quantization has been largely heuristic. This proposal formulates a precise, mathematically grounded quantization scheme by integrating the spectral action principle with the zeta-regularized path integral techniques Connes has developed, treating the Dirac operator itself as the fundamental quantum field.

proposals.title: Non-Perturbative Lattice Approximation of Spectral Actions and Zeta Spectral Triples
proposals.motivation: The spectral action principle, derived from spectral triples, provides a profound geometric formulation of fundamental physics, including the Standard Model. However, its evaluation typically relies on asymptotic expansions, limiting access to the full non-perturbative regime crucial for understanding phenomena like quantum gravity phase transitions. A direct, non-perturbative computation of the spectral action is a major open problem in noncommutative geometry.
proposals.hypothesis: By discretizing the data of a spectral triple onto a finite lattice and employing numerical techniques from lattice quantum field theory, one can compute the associated spectral action and zeta functions non-perturbatively, revealing features inaccessible to heat kernel expansions.
proposals.method: We will construct a finite approximation of a spectral triple (A, H, D) by replacing the infinite-dimensional Hilbert space H with a finite-dimensional one and the Dirac operator D with a large, finite matrix, inspired by the fuzzy geometry and matrix model frameworks. The spectral action Tr(f(D/Λ)) will be computed exactly for this finite system via eigenvalue summation. We will implement Monte Carlo sampling methods, treating the matrix elements of D (or the algebra generators) as dynamical variables with a weight given by exp(-S), where S is the computed spectral action, to explore the non-perturbative configuration space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first numerical, non-perturbative toolkit for analyzing spectral actions, potentially validating the spectral action principle beyond perturbation theory. It could offer new insights into the quantization of geometric spaces and serve as a bridge between the Connes-Chamseddine spectral model of gravity and lattice gauge theory techniques.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis of Connes' spectral formalism with the non-perturbative, computational machinery of lattice field theory represents a significant methodological crossover, moving beyond analytic asymptotic methods to a fully numerical geometric framework.

proposals.title: Finite-Dimensional Spectral Action and the Zeta-Regularized Trace
proposals.motivation: The spectral action principle, a cornerstone of noncommutative geometry, expresses the physical Lagrangian of a theory as the spectrum of a Dirac-type operator. While powerful, its explicit computation for infinite-dimensional spectral triples is analytically complex. Finite-dimensional matrix models offer a concrete testbed, but a robust, principled method for deriving a finite-dimensional spectral action that faithfully approximates the zeta-regularized infinite-dimensional theory is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: For a suitable finite-dimensional matrix approximation of a spectral triple, the zeta-regularized spectral action can be recovered as a limit of a sequence of explicitly computable functionals built from the finite-dimensional spectra and their associated zeta functions.
proposals.method: We will construct finite-dimensional approximations of canonical spectral triples (e.g., the Riemann sphere, noncommutative tori) by truncating the spectral triple to the eigenspaces of the Dirac operator below a cutoff Λ, yielding finite matrix algebras and Dirac matrices. The core algorithm will compute the spectral zeta function ζ_D(s) = Tr(|D|^{-s}) for these finite matrices via their eigenvalues, analytically continue it, and evaluate the associated spectral action S = Tr(f(D/Λ)) using the heat kernel expansion linked to ζ_D(0) and its derivatives. We will systematically compare this to the direct matrix trace of f(D/Λ) and study the convergence as the matrix size increases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous computational framework for the spectral action in finite-dimensional models, directly linking Connes' zeta spectral triple formalism to numerical simulation. It will clarify the regularization process in approximations and potentially offer new insights into the spectral origin of the Standard Model Lagrangian from matrix models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the zeta-regularization framework, central to infinite-dimensional spectral triples, to define and control the spectral action for their finite matrix approximations, rather than treating the finite model action as an ad-hoc truncation.

proposals.title: Spectral Triples on Fractal Base Spaces: A Zeta-Regularized Framework for Non-Integer Dimensions
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the spectral triple formalism encodes geometric data of a manifold via a Dirac operator. This has been profoundly linked to number theory through spectral zeta functions and the Riemann zeros. However, the base space is traditionally a smooth manifold of integer dimension. Exploring fractal base spaces, with their non-integer Hausdorff dimension and spectral complexity, could provide a novel geometric substrate for encoding more intricate arithmetic data and understanding the spectral nature of dimension itself.
proposals.hypothesis: That a spectral triple can be rigorously constructed on a suitable fractal base space (e.g., a Sierpinski gasket or a quantum sphere of non-integer dimension) using zeta-regularization techniques, and that the associated spectral zeta function will exhibit novel poles whose residues define a complex-valued or dimension-interpolating geometric invariant.
proposals.method: The approach will adapt Connes's recent work on 'Zeta Spectral Triples,' where the Dirac operator is rescaled to ensure well-defined zeta functions. We will select a fractal with spectral decimation (like fractafolds) or a noncommutative fractal space defined via a C*-algebra. The Dirac-type operator will be constructed from the existing Laplacian or a derivation calculus, and its spectrum will be analyzed. The core technical step is to apply the zeta-regularization framework to define the dimension spectrum and compute the residue Dixmier trace, generalizing the noncommutative integral to non-integer dimensions.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the axioms of noncommutative geometry to a class of singular spaces, providing a new toolkit for analyzing fractal geometries via operator algebras. It may yield a new class of spectral zeta functions whose pole structure could model phenomena in critical systems or offer a fresh perspective on the dimensional regularization used in physics. The project aims to forge a deeper link between geometric analysis on fractals and number-theoretic zeta functions.
proposals.novelty: While spectral triples on quantum groups and noncommutative spaces exist, the systematic construction on a classical fractal base manifold, with a focus on its zeta-dimension and spectral action, is unexplored. The novelty lies in merging the fractal spectral theory of Kigami-Lapidus with the zeta-regularized framework of Connes-Moscovici.

proposals.title: Modular Classification of Real Structures in Spectral Triples via Tomita-Takesaki Theory
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the real structure J in a spectral triple is a fundamental but axiomatically prescribed component, crucial for defining spin geometry and Poincaré duality. Its deeper origin and classification remain elusive. Pre-2026, Connes' work on zeta spectral triples and the interplay between modular theory (from von Neumann algebras) and spectral invariants suggests a profound, unexplored link: the modular automorphism group from a weight on the algebra may intrinsically generate or constrain J.
proposals.hypothesis: The real structure J in a spectral triple of KO-dimension n is not an independent axiom but is canonically induced, up to a cocycle, by the modular automorphism group of a distinguished weight (e.g., the dual weight from the zeta function), with the KO-dimension imposing specific constraints on the modular invariant.
proposals.method: We will construct a systematic framework by first studying the modular automorphism group σ_t^φ associated to a weight φ on the underlying C*-algebra of a spectral triple. The core technical step is to analyze the commutation relations between σ_t^φ and the Dirac operator D, and the antilinear involution J, using Connes' cocycle Radon-Nikodym theorem. We will classify possible real structures by solving for J satisfying Jσ_t^φ = σ_{-t}^φ J and the spectral triple axioms, particularly the first-order condition. This will be applied to explicit examples: the noncommutative torus, the Riemann zeta spectral triple, and the recently proposed q-series models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a classification theorem for real structures based on modular data, reducing the axiomatic input in noncommutative geometry. It will deepen the understanding of the 'spectral paradigm' by showing how key geometric structures emerge dynamically from operator-algebraic data. Furthermore, it may yield new spectral invariants related to the modular period and offer a fresh perspective on the Riemann zeros through the lens of modular dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While Tomita-Takesaki theory is foundational in von Neumann algebras, its direct application to classify the real structure J in spectral triples is novel. This moves beyond using modular theory solely for state-dependent perturbations (as in twisted spectral triples) to derive the fundamental charge conjugation operator itself.

proposals.title: Clifford Algebras, Spinor Representations, and the Systematic Construction of the J-Involution in Spectral Triples
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the real structure J is a crucial component of a spectral triple, encoding the charge conjugation operator and the KR-cycle of real K-homology. Its construction, however, often appears ad hoc, especially for singular or fractal spaces. A systematic, representation-theoretic foundation for J would deepen the axiomatic framework and clarify its link to the Clifford algebra structure inherent in the Dirac operator.
proposals.hypothesis: The representation theory of real Clifford algebras, governed by the Atiyah-Bott-Shapiro periodicity and the classification of irreducible modules, provides a universal algebraic framework to derive the properties and constraints of the real structure J, particularly its sign table and commutation relations with D and π.
proposals.method: We will formalize the Dirac operator's symbol as generating a Clifford action, linking the metric dimension to a specific Clifford algebra Cl(p,q). The J-operator will be constructed as an intertwiner between the fundamental representation and its dual, or as part of the pinor representation, using the classification of real spinor modules. This will be applied to reconstruct known Js for classical commutative manifolds and then to extend the construction to the 'zeta spectral triple' framework, where the Dirac is built from local zeta functions, and to related arithmetic examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, algebraic-topological prescription for the real structure, reducing its specification to the choice of a Clifford module. It will yield new criteria for the existence of real structures on exotic spectral triples and potentially reveal new invariants from the representation data, strengthening the bridge between noncommutative geometry and topology.
proposals.novelty: While Clifford algebras are known to underpin spin geometry, their systematic use to *derive* the J-involution—rather than just motivate its sign rules—from first principles within the spectral triple formalism is a novel, unifying approach not fully developed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: From Spectral Action to Feynman Rules: A Cyclic Cohomological Derivation of Standard Model Perturbation Theory
proposals.motivation: The Connes-Chamseddine spectral action provides a profound geometric formulation of the Standard Model coupled to gravity, deriving the full Lagrangian from a simple spectral principle. However, the bridge to the computational engine of particle physics—the perturbative expansion governed by Feynman rules—remains formal and indirect. Establishing a direct, algorithmic link is crucial to demonstrate that noncommutative geometry (NCG) not only encodes the action but also the complete perturbative dynamics, offering a deeper geometric origin for quantum field theory (QFT) calculations.
proposals.hypothesis: The cyclic cocycles arising from the heat kernel expansion of the spectral triple for the Standard Model, computed via the noncommutative residue and higher residues, encode the combinatorial data necessary to algorithmically generate the propagators and vertex factors of the corresponding QFT's Feynman rules.
proposals.method: We will systematically compute the terms of the spectral action's asymptotic expansion using the Connes-Moscovici local index formula and the associated cyclic cocycles. This involves a detailed analysis of the Dirac operator of the product geometry $M \times F_{SM}$, separating the finite spectral triple data. We will then construct a precise dictionary mapping specific algebraic structures within these cocycles (e.g., traces over Clifford algebras and finite matrix algebras) to the momentum-space Feynman rules for gauge bosons, Higgs, and fermions. The adversarial safeguard is to validate this dictionary by requiring it to reproduce known physical amplitudes, such as the electron-muon scattering amplitude in the unitary gauge, at each stage of the derivation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first rigorous, step-by-step derivation of Standard Model Feynman rules directly from the spectral action principle, solidifying the link between NCG and perturbative QFT. It will provide a concrete computational schema, turning the spectral model's algebraic data into a generative framework for diagrammatic calculus, thereby making NCG predictions more directly testable against collider data.
proposals.novelty: While the spectral action's Lagrangian is known, deriving the full perturbative expansion rules via cyclic cochomology—treating Feynman diagrams as outputs of a spectral residue calculus—is a novel and unexplored direction that moves beyond the action principle to the dynamics of interactions.

proposals.title: A Riemann–Hilbert Dictionary for Zero-Dimensional QFT: From Feynman Graphs to Algebraic Cycles via Spectral Triples
proposals.motivation: A deep, constructive bridge between perturbative quantum field theory (QFT) and noncommutative geometry (NCG) remains elusive. The zero-dimensional QFT case provides a fully computable combinatorial laboratory where Feynman graphs yield concrete sums, yet their underlying geometric content—potentially encoded as algebraic cycles or cyclic cocycles—is obscured. Establishing an explicit dictionary here is a critical first step to rigorously exporting Connes's vision of a QFT/NCG correspondence to physically relevant higher-dimensional theories.
proposals.hypothesis: The perturbative expansion of a zero-dimensional QFT can be encoded in a Riemann–Hilbert problem whose Stokes data and monodromy correspond precisely to (co)homological invariants (e.g., algebraic cycles or cyclic cocycles) of an associated noncommutative space defined via a spectral triple.
proposals.method: We will construct the dictionary by: 1) Defining a 'zeta spectral triple' for the zero-dimensional theory, where the partition function's asymptotic expansion is linked to the spectral zeta function. 2) Formulating a Riemann–Hilbert problem associated to this expansion, following the framework of exact WKB and resurgence theory (pre-2026). 3) Explicitly computing the Stokes data and monodromy from the Feynman graph expansion. 4) Interpreting this data as a cyclic cocycle in the sense of Connes–Moscovici, or as an algebraic cycle via the Hochschild homology of the relevant 'graph algebra'.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first fully explicit, computable prototype translating QFT perturbation theory into noncommutative geometric invariants. It will provide a new combinatorial interpretation of Stokes phenomena and test the robustness of the spectral triple formalism for QFT. The resulting dictionary will serve as a foundational template for attacking the higher-dimensional case.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, constructive fusion of the Riemann–Hilbert framework—a tool from exact analysis and differential equations—with the abstract algebraic geometry of cycles and the operator-algebraic framework of spectral triples, applied to the concrete combinatorics of zero-dimensional QFT.

proposals.title: Hopf-Cohomological Quantization of Anomalies via Spectral Zeta Invariants
proposals.motivation: Quantum anomalies, which signal the breaking of classical symmetries upon quantization, are central obstructions in quantum field theory and number theory, often described via index theorems or BRST cohomology. Recent work by Connes and others has revealed deep connections between the zeros of zeta functions, the geometry of foliations, and the structure of Hopf algebras, suggesting a unified algebraic framework underlying these phenomena. This proposal seeks to formalize this connection to provide a purely cohomological and operator-algebraic characterization of anomalies, moving beyond case-specific computations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the combined Hopf algebraic structure governing the Connes-Kreimer renormalization Hopf algebra and the Hopf algebra of transverse geometry of foliations provides a universal cohomological framework in which quantum anomalies appear as specific classes in a cyclic cohomology group, directly computable from spectral zeta residues of associated Dirac-type operators.
proposals.method: We will construct a 'zeta spectral triple' (as in Connes's recent work) for the relevant physical or arithmetic configuration space, encoding its symmetries. The combined Hopf algebra action on this triple will be analyzed using cyclic cohomology, particularly the Hopf-cyclic cohomology theory developed up to 2025. The key technical step is to define a cochain map from the Hopf-cyclic complex to a complex built from residues of the spectral zeta function of the Dirac operator, following the local index formula. Anomalies will be identified with non-trivial cocycles in this construction, and their quantification will be via explicit formulas involving zeta-regularized traces.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a unified, cohomological formula for anomalies applicable across quantum field theory, number theory (e.g., in class field theory), and noncommutative geometry. It will directly link the analytic data of spectral zeta functions to algebraic obstruction theory, providing a new tool for classifying and quantifying anomalous behavior in a wide range of systems.
proposals.novelty: While Hopf algebras are known in renormalization and cyclic cohomology is used in index theory, their combined application to provide a universal, computable cohomological home for anomalies—explicitly tied to zeta spectral invariants—is a novel synthesis of Connes's pre-2026 research themes.

proposals.title: Spectral Lie Algebras of Infinitesimal Characters: A Noncommutative Geometric Approach to Renormalization and Zeta Phenomena
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, Hopf algebras of Feynman graphs and of number-theoretic origin provide a unified framework for renormalization in quantum field theory and for the study of zeta functions. The dual Lie algebra of infinitesimal characters governs the dynamics of perturbative expansions and is linked to the Connes–Kreimer beta-function. A systematic spectral and geometric study of these Lie structures, particularly those arising from the 'Zeta Spectral Triples' framework, is needed to uncover new links between physics and arithmetic.
proposals.hypothesis: The Lie algebras of infinitesimal characters associated to the Hopf algebras in Connes's recent work possess a rich spectral geometry (e.g., a natural 'spectral triple' structure) whose invariants encode physical renormalization group flows and arithmetic properties of zeta zeros.
proposals.method: We will explicitly construct the Lie algebra of infinitesimal characters for the Hopf algebras featured in 'Zeta Spectral Triples' and related papers. Using Connes's spectral triple framework, we will equip these infinite-dimensional Lie algebras with a Dirac operator derived from the underlying cyclic homology and the 'q-series' moment problem data. The spectral action principle and the associated zeta functions will be computed to derive beta-functions and flow equations. The connection to class field theory and knots will be explored via the associated representation theory and Milnor invariants of the Lie algebra.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new spectral-geometric characterization of the renormalization group as a flow on a specific Lie algebra. It aims to derive explicit formulas linking the spectrum of the Dirac operator on this Lie algebra to the zeros of the Riemann zeta function and other L-functions, providing a fresh perspective on the Hilbert–Pólya idea. The results will deepen the noncommutative geometric dictionary between quantum physics and number theory.
proposals.novelty: While the Connes–Kreimer Hopf algebra is well-studied, its dual Lie algebra has not been systematically investigated as the carrier of a *spectral triple*. The novelty lies in applying the full machinery of noncommutative geometry (spectral triples, cyclic cohomology) directly to this Lie algebra to probe zeta zeros and renormalization simultaneously.

proposals.title: Quantum Spectral Corrections to the Compactification Map in Noncommutative Geometry
proposals.motivation: In Matrix theory and noncommutative geometry, the compactification map relates higher-dimensional spectral data to lower-dimensional physics, classically recovering spaces and field theories. However, this correspondence is fundamentally classical, ignoring quantum fluctuations inherent in the noncommutative structure. A rigorous incorporation of these effects is necessary to understand the quantum geometry of spacetime and to connect with quantum gravity models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that quantum corrections to the compactification map can be systematically encoded via a deformation of the spectral triple and its associated zeta function, leading to a modified, 'quantum' spectral action whose low-energy expansion includes novel interaction terms.
proposals.method: We will employ Connes' framework of spectral triples and zeta functions. Starting from a spectral triple for a noncommutative toroidal fibration, we will introduce quantum fluctuations as a perturbation of the Dirac operator, modeled via a q-deformation or a random matrix ensemble. The core technical step is to compute the perturbed spectral zeta function and its residues, deriving the quantum-corrected compactification map by analyzing the asymptotic expansion of the associated heat kernel. This will be implemented using symbolic computation for the explicit series expansions of the deformed spectral action.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-principles derivation of quantum corrections to the geometry of compactification in Matrix theory. It will extend the spectral action principle to a quantum regime and potentially reveal new couplings between geometry and matter fields arising from noncommutativity at a fundamental scale.
proposals.novelty: While spectral triples model classical spaces, their direct use to derive systematic quantum corrections to the compactification map from within the noncommutative framework is unexplored. The novelty lies in treating the quantum effects not as an external input but as a deformation of the spectral data itself.

proposals.title: Anabelian Reconstruction of the Noncommutative Boundary: A Spectral Triadic Approach to the Zeta Landscape
proposals.motivation: The Riemann zeta function's zeros encode deep arithmetic information, and Connes's spectral triple framework provides a noncommutative geometric (NCG) model for their study. A key conceptual gap is understanding the 'boundary' of this NCG space, which should relate to the adelic class group and the absolute Galois group. Pre-2026, anabelian geometry, pioneered by Grothendieck and developed by Mochizuki, shows that certain schemes are uniquely determined by their étale fundamental groups, offering a powerful tool to reconstruct arithmetic from topology.
proposals.hypothesis: The étale fundamental group of the noncommutative boundary space, constructed from the spectral action of the adele class group, can be reconstructed anabelianly from its associated spectral zeta data, and this reconstruction reveals a new correspondence between Galois symmetries and the distribution of zeta zeros.
proposals.method: We will first construct a precise 'boundary' object within Connes's topos-theoretic framework for the adele class space, defining its étale fundamental groupoid in a noncommutative sense. Using techniques from 'Zeta Spectral Triples', we will encode its structure into a system of `L`-functions and spectral data. The core anabelian method will involve adapting Mochizuki's mono-anabelian reconstruction algorithms to this NCG setting, aiming to show that the ring of adelic functions invariant under the spectral action can be recovered from the profinite completion of this fundamental group. This will be tested by comparing the resulting symmetries with the action of the Weil group on the zeros.
proposals.contribution: This work will forge a new link between anabelian geometry and noncommutative geometry, providing a topological interpretation of the spectral realization of zeta zeros. It aims to produce a new invariant—the anabelian fundamental group of the boundary—that could classify different spectral realizations and offer a fresh perspective on the global `L`-functions associated to number fields.
proposals.novelty: While anabelian geometry has been applied to schemes and Galois groups, its application to the fundamentally noncommutative and analytic 'boundary' of the adele class space is entirely unexplored. The novelty lies in using topological reconstruction algorithms to decode spectral data, a synthesis of Mochizuki's arithmetic methods with Connes's operator-algebraic framework.

proposals.title: Noncommutative Boundary Entropy and Information Flow in Zeta Spectral Triples
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the boundary of a spectral triple is a critical structure, encoding dynamics and singularities. Pre-2026 work by Connes and others on zeta spectral triples and the Bost-Connes system suggests deep links between operator algebras, number theory, and dynamics. However, a quantitative information-theoretic analysis of the noncommutative boundary, particularly its capacity to process or obscure information, remains undeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamical entropy (specifically, Connes-Narnhofer-Thirring entropy or Voiculescu's topological entropy) of the time evolution on the boundary algebra of a zeta spectral triple captures intrinsic geometric and arithmetic information, and that this entropy is related to the analytic properties of the associated L-functions.
proposals.method: The approach will construct explicit zeta spectral triples for arithmetic models, such as those arising from function fields or the Bost-Connes system. We will compute the modular automorphism group on the boundary algebra and apply the CNT entropy formula. A key technical step involves analyzing the asymptotic commutant to define a suitable entropy invariant, leveraging the spectral framework and the moment problem techniques from Connes's recent work on local factors. This will be complemented by studying the flow of information via relative entropy between states on the boundary.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new entropy invariant for noncommutative boundaries, offering a quantitative tool to classify singular dynamical systems in noncommutative geometry. It aims to establish a novel bridge between operator algebraic entropy, the distribution of zeros of zeta functions, and class field theory, potentially revealing a thermodynamic interpretation of arithmetic.
proposals.novelty: While entropy has been studied in operator algebras, its systematic application to the boundary of spectral triples, especially those linked to zeta functions, is novel. The proposal uniquely combines Connes's recent spectral/zeta framework with deep concepts from quantum information theory to probe arithmetic dynamics.

proposals.title: Non-Perturbative Resummation in the Diffeomorphism Group and Hopf Algebra Framework: Towards a Spectral Model of Zeta Zeros
proposals.motivation: In quantum field theory and renormalization, perturbative expansions are formal power series whose non-perturbative completion is a central, unresolved problem. Alain Connes's recent work on 'Zeta Spectral Triples' and the Hopf algebra framework for diffeomorphisms provides a novel geometric and algebraic setting to study this problem, potentially linking it to the spectral interpretation of Riemann zeta zeros.
proposals.hypothesis: That the action of the diffeomorphism group on the space of formal power series, encoded in a specific Hopf algebra, admits a natural non-perturbative resummation procedure defined via a spectral triple, and that the spectrum of the associated Dirac operator can model the statistical distribution of zeta function zeros.
proposals.method: We will construct a spectral triple where the algebra is the Hopf algebra of rooted trees (or Feynman diagrams) related to the diffeomorphism group, the Hilbert space is built from formal series, and the Dirac operator is designed to 'measure' the growth of coefficients. Using Connes's 'q-series' and moment problem techniques, we will define a regularized trace (Dixmier trace) of the heat kernel exp(-tD^2) to extract a zeta function. The resummation will be achieved by analyzing the singularities of the Borel transform within this spectral framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous operator-algebraic and geometric formulation of Borel–Écalle–type resummation, moving it beyond formal analysis. It aims to produce a concrete, testable spectral model where the low-lying spectrum of a Dirac operator approximates the Riemann zeros, offering a new avenue in the Hilbert–Pólya program via renormalization theory.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of perturbative renormalization Hopf algebras with the non-commutative geometry of diffeomorphisms to address non-perturbative completion is unexplored. Applying the 'Zeta Spectral Triple' formalism to the resummation problem itself, rather than to number-theoretic zeta functions directly, is a novel inversion of the methodology.

proposals.title: Spectral Triples with Infinite-Dimensional Internal Spaces: Beyond Almost Commutative Geometry
proposals.motivation: In Noncommutative Geometry, the almost commutative manifold (product of a spin manifold with a finite matrix algebra) has been foundational for particle physics models. However, this assumption restricts the internal 'space' to a zero-dimensional object, limiting the geometric richness and potential physical applications. A framework allowing for infinite-dimensional internal algebras is a crucial step towards more realistic quantum geometric models and a deeper understanding of spectral invariants like zeta functions.
proposals.hypothesis: That one can construct consistent, physically motivated spectral triples where the internal algebra is not finite-dimensional (e.g., a C*-algebra of compact operators, a UHF algebra, or a crossed product) while retaining key analytic properties like summability and a well-defined spectral action principle.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage Connes' recent work on 'Zeta Spectral Triples' and the spectral characterization of primes. We will systematically replace the finite matrix algebra F with infinite-dimensional, non-type I operator algebras. The core technical task is to define a Dirac operator on the product structure that interacts correctly with the infinite internal space, ensuring the resulting spectral triple remains (θ-)summable. We will then compute the associated spectral zeta function and explore the convergence and residues of the corresponding spectral action, using the moment problem techniques from recent work on local L-factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the axiomatic framework of Noncommutative Geometry to a new class of models, breaking the 'zero-dimensionality' constraint of internal spaces. It will provide new candidate spectral triples for exploring links between operator algebras, number theory (via spectral zeta zeros), and fundamental physics, potentially offering novel geometric origins for field theoretic structures.
proposals.novelty: While infinite-dimensional algebras are studied in operator theory, their integration into the full package of a spectral triple with a non-trivial, *non-finite* internal space and a computable spectral action moves beyond the established almost commutative paradigm and its direct generalizations.

proposals.title: Spectral Derivation of Particle Physics from Axiomatic Noncommutative Geometry
proposals.motivation: The Connes-Chamseddine spectral action principle successfully derived the Standard Model Lagrangian from a specific noncommutative geometry (the finite space $F$). However, this geometry was chosen to match known physics. The deeper question, central to pre-2026 noncommutative geometry, is whether the axioms of the theory themselves necessitate the emergence of a structure isomorphic to the Standard Model's gauge group and fermionic content, thereby inverting the logical order of derivation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that imposing fundamental axioms of noncommutative geometry—specifically, the conditions for a real, even spectral triple with Poincaré duality and a noncommutative manifold structure of metric dimension 4—uniquely determines, up to finite algebraic constraints, the finite algebra and its representation, which must correspond to the Standard Model.
proposals.method: We will employ the spectral triple framework and the classification of finite spectral triples. Starting from the general axioms, we will systematically derive constraints on the finite algebra $\mathcal{A}_F = C^\infty(M) \otimes \mathcal{A}_{F}^{\,0}$. Using K-theoretic (KK-theory) and homological conditions (Poincaré duality in cyclic cohomology), we will restrict the possible $\mathcal{A}_{F}^{\,0}$ and their representations on the Hilbert space. The moment problem associated to local zeta factors, as explored in Connes's recent work, will be used to analyze the spectral action's bosonic sector and constrain the gauge group. The goal is a categorical derivation of the algebra $\mathbb{C} \oplus \mathbb{H} \oplus M_3(\mathbb{C})$.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide a foundational justification for the Standard Model from first geometric principles, moving it from a phenomenological fit to a mathematical necessity within the NCG framework. It would also refine the axiomatization of noncommutative manifolds and potentially reveal new constraints on particle physics (e.g., the number of generations) from deeper topological requirements.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reversing the logic of the spectral construction: instead of building a geometry to match physics, we derive the physics as the unique solution to a set of abstract geometric axioms. This shifts the focus from application to a fundamental classification theorem within noncommutative geometry.

proposals.title: Inverse Spectral Reconstruction of Particle Physics from Zeta Spectral Triples
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the spectral data of a Dirac operator on a spectral triple encodes the geometry of an underlying space. The Standard Model coupled to gravity has been formulated as a spectral triple, suggesting particle physics data (masses, mixing angles, couplings) are spectral outputs. The inverse problem—deducing the geometric and algebraic structure of the noncommutative space from this observed data—remains a profound challenge, central to understanding physics as geometry.
proposals.hypothesis: The experimentally measured parameters of the Standard Model, interpreted as spectral invariants, uniquely constrain the finite noncommutative algebra and Dirac operator of the associated spectral triple up to unitary equivalence, revealing a specific geometric fingerprint.
proposals.method: We will employ the framework of zeta spectral triples, where the zeta function of the Dirac operator is regular at zero. The method involves: 1) Formulating the particle physics data as constraints on the spectrum and residues of the spectral zeta function. 2) Using the moment problem associated to local factors (from recent work on q-series) to reconstruct the spectral measure. 3) Applying Connes' reconstruction theorem for spectral triples and tools from class field theory to deduce the possible finite-dimensional real $C^*$-algebra (the 'internal space') and the Yukawa coupling matrix as a Dirac operator component.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, bottom-up procedure to derive candidate noncommutative geometries from empirical data, moving beyond model-building. It will establish new links between number-theoretic aspects of zeta functions and phenomenological constraints, potentially identifying a unique geometric origin for the Standard Model's algebraic structure.
proposals.novelty: While the forward construction of particle models from geometry is established, the systematic inverse problem using zeta-regularized spectral data and the moment problem is novel. It inverts the traditional approach, treating physics as the primary spectral data to be explained.

proposals.title: Spectral Triples and Zeta Functions over Non-*-Algebras: A Framework for Generalized Riemann Zeros
proposals.motivation: The foundational framework of noncommutative geometry (NCG), particularly spectral triples, is built on *-algebras of bounded operators. This structure is essential for defining a real structure (charge conjugation) and a fundamental symmetry like Poincaré duality. However, this constraint may exclude rich algebraic structures arising in number-theoretic contexts, such as certain Hecke algebras or algebras defined by q-series, which are naturally non-*-algebras but are linked to zeta functions and L-factors, as seen in Connes's recent work on local factors and class field theory.
proposals.hypothesis: That a consistent theory of 'non-*-spectral triples' can be formulated, where the Dirac operator's spectrum encodes arithmetic data, and that the absence of a *-involution will correspond to a breakdown of a specific symmetry, revealing new spectral interpretations of zeta zeros and local L-factors.
proposals.method: We will first axiomatize a 'zeta spectral triple' (A, H, D) where A is a non-*-algebra, possibly a specific q-deformed ring or a non-self-adjoint Hecke operator algebra. The focus will be on the spectral zeta function ζ_D(s)=Tr(|D|^{-s}) and its phase generalisation. Using the moment problem framework associated to local factors, we will classify the possible spectral measures. The Connes-Moscovici local index formula will be re-examined in this setting, replacing the *-structure with a weaker condition linked to a twisted trace or a derivation, potentially inspired by the cyclic cohomology of non-*-algebras.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the scope of noncommutative geometry to encompass algebras central to number theory that lack a natural involution. It aims to produce a new spectral model for zeta zeros not necessarily tied to a Hermitian operator, and provide a unified algebraic framework for the q-series and local factor constructions studied in recent pre-2026 papers.
proposals.novelty: While noncommutative geometry is deeply tied to C*-algebras, systematically abandoning the *-involution as a foundational axiom is a radical departure. The novelty lies in leveraging this relaxation to directly incorporate non-self-adjoint operators and algebras that appear naturally in arithmetic, moving beyond the traditional spectral interpretation of the Riemann Hypothesis.

proposals.title: Spectral Triples and Zeta Invariants over Banach and Fréchet Modules
proposals.motivation: The standard framework of noncommutative geometry, built on spectral triples with Hilbert space representations, is insufficient for many analytic and number-theoretic contexts where natural function spaces (e.g., certain L^p spaces, spaces of test functions) are not Hilbertian. Recent work on 'Zeta Spectral Triples' and connections to local factors suggests that a more flexible representation theory is needed to capture finer spectral data and to model geometries linked to L-functions and q-series.
proposals.hypothesis: That a rigorous theory of spectral triples can be constructed on Banach or Fréchet modules, preserving the core spectral and metric data, and that this extended framework will yield new spectral invariants (e.g., zeta residues, determinants) sensitive to the underlying representation space, applicable to problems in analytic number theory and knot theory.
proposals.method: The approach will systematically generalize the axioms of a spectral triple (A, H, D) by replacing the Hilbert space H with a suitable topological module (e.g., a Banach A-module or a Fréchet space) and reinterpreting the compact resolvent condition and summability requirements within this context.
