Key technical steps involve: 1) Defining an appropriate notion of 'Dirac operator' as an unbounded operator on such modules with suitable spectral properties. 2) Developing a noncommutative integration theory and zeta-regularization for the associated spectral functions (Trace(|D|^{-s})) in this generalized setting. 3) Testing the framework on concrete examples arising from recent pre-2026 work, such as the C*-dynamical systems associated to q-series or the geometric data linked to local factors of L-functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the applicability of noncommutative geometry to a broader class of function spaces and operator algebras, directly relevant to Connes's recent investigations into primes and knots. It will provide a new tool for constructing and analyzing spectral models of arithmetic objects where Hilbert space structures are unnatural or unavailable.
proposals.novelty: While Banach space techniques exist in operator algebra, a systematic reformulation of the foundational data of noncommutative geometry (spectral triples) on non-Hilbertian spaces, with a focus on generating new spectral zeta invariants, is a novel and unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Spectral Supersymmetry: Deriving Spectral Triples and Zeta Functions from Supersymmetric Quantum Mechanical Algebras
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the spectral triple (A, H, D) is foundational, with the algebra A and Dirac operator D being primary. Supersymmetric quantum mechanics (SUSY QM) provides a natural framework for spectral triples, but its algebraic structure is typically derived from the given data. Inverting this relationship could reveal a more fundamental, symmetry-driven origin for the geometry of spaces, including those relevant to number theory where spectral triples for the Riemann zeta function are sought.
proposals.hypothesis: The algebraic data of a noncommutative space—specifically, the function algebra and the Dirac operator of a spectral triple—can be systematically reconstructed from a given supersymmetry algebra, treating the latter as the primary, defining structure.
proposals.method: We will formalize the SUSY algebra, defined by operators Q, Q†, H = {Q, Q†}, and its grading Γ, as the initial input. Within Connes' framework, we will develop a reconstruction theorem: the 'algebra of observables' A will be derived as a suitable subalgebra of operators on H that (anti)commute with the SUSY data in a specified manner. The Dirac operator D will be identified from the internal structure of H = Q + Q†. This procedure will be tested on known SUSY QM examples (e.g., on the circle) and then applied to construct candidate spectral triples for the Riemann zeros by positing a SUSY algebra whose Witten index and spectral asymmetry encode the zeta function's properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, symmetry-first axiomatic approach to constructing spectral triples, potentially simplifying the search for a spectral realization of the Riemann zeros. It will deepen the connection between noncommutative geometry and supersymmetric physics, offering a fresh perspective on how algebraic structures emerge from fundamental symmetries.
proposals.novelty: While SUSY QM is known to exemplify spectral triples, the systematic inversion—deriving the triple's algebra and operator from a priori SUSY data—is an unexplored foundational shift, moving from geometry-derived symmetries to symmetry-derived geometry.

proposals.title: Spectral Triples and Zeta-Regularized Determinants for Explicit Superstring Vacua
proposals.motivation: The classification of superstring vacua, particularly those with non-geometric fluxes, remains a central challenge in string theory. Alain Connes' non-commutative geometry (NCG) provides a rigorous mathematical framework to describe spaces where standard geometry fails, which is precisely the regime relevant for such exotic vacua. The recent development of 'Zeta Spectral Triples' offers a new tool to encode the spectral data of these spaces, potentially linking their operator-algebraic structure to physical partition functions.
proposals.hypothesis: That specific classes of superstring vacua, particularly those arising from non-geometric Q-flux backgrounds or certain Gepner-type models, can be constructed as spectral triples whose associated spectral zeta functions compute the one-loop vacuum amplitudes and whose dimension spectrum reveals the underlying number-theoretic structure.
proposals.method: We will construct explicit spectral triples for candidate vacua by defining a suitable non-commutative algebra (e.g., derived from a crossed product by a duality twist or a quantum group symmetry), a Hilbert space of spinors, and a Dirac operator whose spectrum encodes the masses of string excitations. The core technical step will be to compute the associated spectral zeta function and its regularized determinant, using the 'Zeta Spectral Triple' framework to ensure convergence. This will be applied to models where the local factors, as studied in Connes' recent work on q-series and moments, correspond to partition functions of internal conformal field theories.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first explicit, mathematically rigorous NCG models for specific superstring vacua, moving beyond analogies to concrete construction. It will provide a new spectral criterion for vacuum stability based on the positivity of the zeta-regularized determinant. Furthermore, it may uncover a direct operator-algebraic origin for the appearance of arithmetic L-functions in string compactifications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of the cutting-edge 'Zeta Spectral Triple' formalism—a tool developed for number theory—to the concrete physics of string vacua, and in the explicit computation of physical amplitudes from the spectral data of a single non-commutative geometric object.

proposals.title: A Spectral Classification of Geometries via Supersymmetry Algebras and Zeta Functions
proposals.motivation: The classification of geometries via their symmetry algebras is a profound problem in mathematics and physics. In noncommutative geometry, spectral triples provide a robust framework for describing geometries, including those beyond the classical continuum. Concurrently, supersymmetric quantum mechanics links spectral data of Dirac operators to topological invariants. A unified classification, grounded in the algebraic structure of supersymmetry, would bridge these domains and offer a universal taxonomy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the complete classification of (possibly noncommutative) geometries encoded by spectral triples can be achieved by analyzing the representation theory of their associated supersymmetry algebras, and that this classification is intrinsically linked to the analytic properties of spectral zeta functions.
proposals.method: We will construct a categorical framework where objects are spectral triples equipped with a supersymmetry algebra (e.g., generated by the Dirac operator and a grading/chirality operator). The classification will proceed by: 1) Characterizing the irreducible modules of these algebras over various coefficient rings (C, R, quaternions). 2) Employing Connes' theory of the spectral action and the associated zeta functions to define invariants (poles, residues, moments) that distinguish geometries within the same algebraic class. 3) Applying this to known examples from the 'Zeta Spectral Triples' program, arithmetic geometries, and knot complements, using the moment problem techniques from recent work on local factors to extract geometric data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a complete taxonomy of geometries based on supersymmetry, providing a new dictionary between operator algebraic data, representation theory, and topology. It will yield new invariants for noncommutative spaces and deepen the understanding of the spectral realization of zeros of zeta functions, potentially offering a fresh perspective on the Riemann hypothesis through geometric classification.
proposals.novelty: While supersymmetry algebras are known in spectral triples, their systematic use as the primary classifying tool for a complete taxonomy is novel. The explicit fusion of this algebraic classification with the detailed analytic data of spectral zeta functions and moment problems represents a significant advance beyond existing, more partial classifications.

proposals.title: Analytic K-Theory and Index Invariants for Zeta Spectral Triples
proposals.motivation: The construction of 'zeta spectral triples' by Connes and collaborators provides a non-commutative geometric framework where the local factors of arithmetic zeta functions are recovered from spectral data of a Dirac-type operator. A key open problem is to fully characterize the invariants encoded by these operators, particularly their relationship to arithmetic K-theory and class field theory, as suggested by recent work on knots and primes.
proposals.hypothesis: The analytic K-theory classes and higher index invariants associated with the Dirac operators of zeta spectral triples carry refined arithmetic information, potentially linking to étale cohomology or the structure of the absolute Galois group, beyond the spectral realization of zeros.
proposals.method: The research will employ Connes' quantized calculus within the established framework of zeta spectral triples. We will compute the cyclic cohomology Chern character of the spectral triple and pair it with the analytic K-theory of the associated non-commutative algebra, using the local index formula. Specifically, we will analyze the residue cocycles arising from the zeta-regularized operators to define new numerical invariants. These calculations will be tested on explicit examples related to function fields and Hecke algebras, where the spectral data is connected to L-functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct K-theoretic interpretation of the zeta spectral triple construction, providing a new bridge between non-commutative index theory and arithmetic. It aims to produce computable, higher-dimensional invariants that could offer a cohomological explanation for the observed spectral correspondences in class field theory.
proposals.novelty: While spectral triples and index theory are classical, their systematic application to the new 'zeta spectral triple' framework to extract K-theoretic arithmetic invariants is unexplored. The novelty lies in fusing analytic K-theory with the specific residue structures inherent to these spectral triples designed for L-factors.

proposals.title: Spectral Convergence of Finite Matrix Approximations to Noncommutative Geometries and Zeta Invariants
proposals.motivation: A central challenge in noncommutative geometry (NCG) is the rigorous approximation of infinite-dimensional spectral triples, which encode geometric and analytic data, by finite-dimensional matrix algebras. This is crucial for computational and conceptual access to deep problems, such as the spectral realization of zeros of L-functions, where the relevant operator algebras are inherently infinite-dimensional. Establishing a robust approximation framework would bridge Connes' spectral paradigm with finite-dimensional operator theory and numerical analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: That for a specific class of spectral triples (e.g., those associated to the adele class space or function fields), there exist natural sequences of finite-dimensional matrix algebras whose spectral data (eigenvalues, zeta functions) converge to the spectral data of the limiting NCG, thereby providing a discrete approximation to the Riemann zeta zeros and other arithmetic spectral invariants.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage Connes' recent work on 'Zeta Spectral Triples' and the 'q-series' moment problem. We will construct explicit sequences of finite-dimensional C*-algebras (e.g., full matrix algebras M_N(C)) equipped with approximating Dirac operators D_N. The convergence will be studied in the spectral sense, analyzing the behavior of Tr(|D_N|^{-s}) as N→∞ and its relation to the limiting spectral zeta function. Key techniques include the theory of spectral truncations, the moment problem for determining D_N from local factors, and the use of the 'spectral' and 'ring' framework to maintain arithmetic structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, computable framework for approximating central objects in NCG, potentially enabling numerical exploration of spectral zeta zeros. It will establish a new link between the theory of operator algebras, approximation theory, and arithmetic geometry, offering a novel pathway to investigate the Riemann hypothesis through finite-dimensional spectral analysis.
proposals.novelty: While matrix approximations exist in operator algebras, a systematic spectral convergence theory tailored to the arithmetic spectral triples central to Connes' Riemann hypothesis program is absent. The novelty lies in using the 'q-series' moment problem to dictate the finite-dimensional approximants, ensuring they preserve the arithmetic meaning of the spectral data.

proposals.title: Spectral Reconstruction of Algebras from Supersymmetric Data: A Dual Approach to Noncommutative Geometry
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, spectral triples encode geometric data of an algebra via a Dirac operator, often linked to supersymmetric quantum mechanics. The standard approach constructs the supersymmetry (the Dirac operator) from a given algebra. The inverse problem—classifying all possible spectral triples (algebras) compatible with a fixed supersymmetry representation—remains largely unexplored but is fundamental for understanding the uniqueness of geometric reconstructions and for physical models where the symmetry is primary.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given supersymmetry algebra (e.g., a fixed Dirac spectrum or a `q`-deformed supercharge), the space of compatible noncommutative algebras forms a non-trivial moduli space, which can be parametrized using techniques from the moment problem and class field theory, revealing constraints analogous to those of the Riemann zeta zeros.
proposals.method: We will employ Connes' framework of spectral triples and zeta functions. Starting from a prescribed spectral datum (e.g., the spectrum of a Dirac operator mimicking the zeros of the Riemann zeta function, or a `q`-series from local factors), we will formulate the reconstruction of the algebra as a moment problem, seeking all positive functionals on a candidate symbol calculus. This will be tackled using operator algebra techniques (C*-algebra classification, K-theory) and refined by analogies to the Frobenius-eigenvalue distributions in 'Knots, primes and class field theory', seeking an arithmetic underpinning for the reconstruction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic dual to the algebra-supersymmetry correspondence, expanding the toolkit of noncommutative geometry. It will yield new insights into the spectral constraints on possible algebras, potentially linking spectral invariants to arithmetic geometry. The results could inform approaches to quantum gravity and number theory where spectral data is fundamental.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the standard direction of construction in noncommutative geometry. While prior work (including Connes' own) builds the spectral triple from an algebra, we start from the spectral/supersymmetric data and solve for the algebra, a problem not systematically addressed in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Spectral Flow and the Commutative Limit: A Systematic Study of Deformation Parameters in Noncommutative Geometry
proposals.motivation: Noncommutative geometry (NCG) provides a powerful framework for unifying disparate mathematical structures and modeling physical theories beyond the Standard Model. However, the precise mechanism by which these quantized geometries reduce to their classical commutative counterparts, as deformation parameters (like those in q-deformations or the Planck length) vanish, remains underexplored. A rigorous analysis of this limit is crucial for validating NCG as a fundamental theory and for understanding the emergence of classical spacetime and number-theoretic structures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the commutative limit of a spectral triple can be systematically controlled via the spectral flow of the Dirac operator, and that the behavior of associated zeta functions and spectral invariants in this limit reveals deep connections between the deformation parameter, the topology of the underlying space, and the distribution of spectral zeros.
proposals.method: The approach will employ the framework of spectral triples and zeta functions. We will construct families of spectral triples parameterized by a deformation parameter ħ (or q), encompassing examples from quantum groups, noncommutative tori, and the 'zeta spectral triple' related to the Riemann zeros. The core technical task is to analyze the asymptotic expansion of the spectral action Tr(f(D_ħ/Λ)) and the associated spectral zeta functions ζ_{D_ħ}(s) as ħ→0. This involves applying perturbative methods from semiclassical analysis to the Dirac operator and studying the convergence of the associated Connes distance formula and Hochschild cycle to their classical counterparts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, general procedure for taking the commutative limit in NCG, clarifying the conditions under which classical geometry and topology are recovered. It will yield new insights into the spectral interpretation of the Riemann zeros by examining the limit of the 'zeta spectral triple,' potentially linking the deformation parameter to the spacing of zeros. The results will strengthen the foundational basis for physical applications of NCG in semiclassical gravity.
proposals.novelty: While individual deformation limits are known, a unified spectral and analytic treatment across different NCG models, explicitly connecting the parameter flow to zeta-regularized invariants and spectral flow, is novel. Applying this systematic analysis to the number-theoretic 'zeta spectral triple' is a new direction.

proposals.title: Spectral Triples and the Diffeomorphism Group of the Primal Locus: A Non-Abelian Class Field Theory
proposals.motivation: In pre-2026 work, notably 'Knots, primes and class field theory', Connes has explored deep analogies between knots and primes, suggesting a geometric space (the 'primal locus') where Galois groups act as diffeomorphisms. Understanding the non-abelian structure of this diffeomorphism group is the critical next step to extend these analogies beyond abelian class field theory, potentially revealing a geometric framework for the Langlands program.
proposals.hypothesis: The group of diffeomorphisms of the primal locus possesses a rich non-abelian structure that can be encoded and studied via a family of spectral triples, whose spectral zeta functions are related to the local L-factors of automorphic representations.
proposals.method: We will construct a canonical spectral triple for the noncommutative space associated to the diffeomorphism group, building on the 'Zeta Spectral Triples' framework. The Dirac operator will be designed to incorporate dynamical data from the group action. We will then compute the associated spectral zeta function and its poles, using Connes' trace formula and the moment problem techniques from recent work on q-series and local factors, to establish a spectral interpretation of the group's representation theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete operator-algebraic model for a non-abelian extension of the arithmetic-geometric correspondences in Connes' earlier work. It aims to establish a direct spectral link between the geometry of the primal locus and the analytic data of non-abelian L-functions, offering a new perspective on noncommutative geometry's role in number theory.
proposals.novelty: While spectral triples have been applied to abelian class field theory, their systematic deployment to capture the full, non-abelian diffeomorphism group of the arithmetic site is novel. The proposal innovatively merges the 'Zeta Spectral Triple' formalism with the dynamical structure of diffeomorphism groups to attack non-abelian class field theory.

proposals.title: Spectral Triples and Zeta Functions for Semi-direct Products of Character Groups
proposals.motivation: The analysis of non-abelian, non-compact Lie groups arising from duals of number-theoretic objects is central to noncommutative geometry and its applications to number theory, particularly the Riemann hypothesis. Pre-2026, Connes' work on spectral triples and zeta functions for quantum statistical mechanics and class field theory suggests that understanding the representation theory and harmonic analysis on groups of the form Ĥ ⋊ Γ is a critical step towards constructing spectral realizations of L-functions and exploring new 'zeta spectral triples'.
proposals.hypothesis: The unitary dual of G = Ĥ ⋊ Γ, for H a locally compact abelian group with arithmetic significance (e.g., adelic or p-adic), can be completely classified via Mackey's machine, and the resulting Plancherel measure will encode arithmetic data related to the zeta functions of the associated dynamical system.
proposals.method: We will employ Mackey theory for semi-direct products as the primary framework. The method involves: 1) Classifying the orbits of the dual action of Γ on the Pontryagin dual Ĥ, focusing on cases where H is the additive group of a local field or a restricted product with number-theoretic relevance. 2) Calculating the stabilizers and their irreducible representations to induce representations of G. 3) Using Connes' prior work on spectral triples and cyclic cohomology to construct a natural spectral triple on the crossed product C*-algebra associated to the group action, where the Dirac operator's zeta function is related to the Plancherel measure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous harmonic analysis foundation for a new class of examples in noncommutative geometry, directly applicable to constructing dynamical systems for class field theory and analyzing local factors of zeta functions. It will yield explicit formulas for the decomposition of the regular representation, generalizing the classical Plancherel theorem to these arithmetic-group contexts.
proposals.novelty: While Mackey theory is classical, its systematic application to these specific arithmetic group structures, combined with the explicit aim of feeding the results into the construction of 'zeta spectral triples', is novel. It bridges detailed representation theory with Connes' spectral model for zeros of L-functions.

proposals.title: Spectral Decomposition of Character Groups via Crystallographic Symmetry-Breaking: A Noncommutative Geometric Framework
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, understanding the structure of groups of characters, such as those arising in class field theory or from the adèle class space, is crucial for spectral analyses of zeta functions. Pre-2026 work by Connes et al. links zeros of zeta functions to spectral properties of operators, but a systematic, geometric framework for decomposing the relevant Lie groups of characters—often semi-direct products—is lacking. Drawing an analogy from the well-established crystallographic decomposition of space groups into translational lattices and point groups provides a powerful, structured methodology to fill this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: The complete structure (Lie algebra, representations, topology) of a semi-direct product Lie group G = N ⋊ H of characters can be systematically determined by classifying all possible smooth actions of H on the normal subgroup N, analogous to classifying all symmetry-compatible actions of a point group on a Bravais lattice in crystallography.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the normal Lie subgroup N (the 'translational' part) using harmonic analysis on its dual. Second, we will classify all Lie group homomorphisms H → Aut(N) (the 'symmetry-breaking' actions) by studying the cohomology of H with coefficients in the automorphism group of the Lie algebra of N. Third, we will synthesize this data to construct the semi-direct sum Lie algebra, compute the unitary dual of G via the Mackey machine, and determine topological invariants using the associated spectral triple framework. This proceeds entirely within the pre-2026 toolkit of noncommutative geometry and Lie theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, decomposition-based analytical plan to replace placeholder constructions in the analysis of spectral triples associated to zeta functions. It will yield a new class of explicitly computable examples of noncommutative spaces whose spectral invariants are directly tied to number-theoretic data, strengthening the bridge between operator algebras, geometry, and arithmetic.
proposals.novelty: The explicit application of the crystallographic decomposition paradigm—a cornerstone of classical symmetry analysis—to the structure theory of Lie groups of characters in noncommutative geometry is novel. It transforms an abstract classification problem into a concrete, stepwise procedure inspired by solid-state physics.

proposals.title: Spectral Triples and the Semi-direct Product of Character Groups: A Framework for Zeta Zeros
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the spectral realization of zeros of L-functions, pioneered by Connes, provides a profound link between operator algebras and number theory. The group G of characters, a semi-direct product arising from the action of the idele class group, is central to this framework but its full structure and spectral implications remain incompletely analyzed. A complete understanding of G is crucial for advancing the spectral model of the Riemann zeta function and other L-functions within the pre-2026 research program.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the semi-direct product structure of the character group G can be fully elucidated using the theory of crossed products of C*-algebras and that this refined structure will yield a new, natural spectral triple whose spectral zeta function encodes arithmetic data more transparently.
proposals.method: We will employ the framework of crossed product C*-algebras to model the semi-direct product structure of G. Using Connes' recent work on 'Zeta Spectral Triples', we will construct a new spectral triple on this crossed product algebra. The method involves calculating the associated zeta function via the explicit Dirac operator and analyzing its residue traces and high-energy asymptotics to link them to the action of G on the adele class space, following the techniques outlined in the 'Knots, primes and class field theory' paper.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a complete, operator-algebraic description of the character group G, solidifying the foundation of the spectral model. It is expected to produce a new, canonical spectral triple whose spectral action principle may offer a fresh perspective on the location of zeta zeros, contributing directly to the noncommutative approach to the Riemann hypothesis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic application of crossed product constructions and the latest 'Zeta Spectral Triple' formalism specifically to the semi-direct product character group G, a synthesis not previously undertaken in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Spectral Triples and Experimental Signatures of Hopf Duality in Quantum Integrable Systems
proposals.motivation: The Hopf algebra duality between the algebra of conserved charges and the algebra of quasiparticle statistics is a profound mathematical structure underpinning quantum integrability, as seen in the quantum group U_q(sl_2) symmetry of the XXZ chain. However, its physical manifestations remain abstract. Pre-2026, Connes' work on spectral triples and zeta functions for noncommutative spaces provides a novel analytical framework to connect this algebraic duality to spectral data of physical observables, bridging a gap between pure mathematical theory and experimental quantum simulation.
proposals.hypothesis: The dual Hopf structure of a quantum integrable model leaves a measurable imprint on its physical correlation spectra, which can be isolated and characterized using the spectral theory of noncommutative geometry, particularly through the construction of adapted spectral triples and their associated zeta functions.
proposals.method: We will construct a 'Hopf spectral triple' for the XXZ model, where the Dirac operator is built from the action of the Hopf algebra generators and its dual on the Hilbert space. The spectral zeta function of this triple will be analyzed for residues and asymptotics linked to the quantum dimension and R-matrix. This spectral data will be explicitly connected to the dynamic structure factor via the Kubo formula and to entanglement entropy via the replica trick, deriving specific, algebraically dictated corrections. Numerical diagonalization (DMRG) and Bethe Ansatz solutions will validate the analytical predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first concrete dictionary between Hopf algebraic invariants (e.g., quantum dimensions, Drinfeld polynomials) and directly measurable spectral quantities in quantum many-body physics. It will deliver specific, falsifiable predictions for cold-atom and condensed matter experiments, while advancing the application of noncommutative geometry to physical systems with quantum group symmetry.
proposals.novelty: The application of Connes' spectral triple formalism to extract *experimental signatures* from Hopf duality is entirely new, moving beyond classification to prediction. It directly connects the abstract 'zeta spectral triple' concept to the dynamical correlation functions of a concrete, physically realized model.

proposals.title: Spectral Duality and Observables: A Categorical Framework from Syntax to Semantics in Noncommutative Geometry
proposals.motivation: In noncommutative geometry, the spectral interpretation of zeros of the Riemann zeta function via Connes' spectral triple framework suggests a deep duality between algebraic structures and their spectral realizations. This mirrors the categorical duality between program syntax and semantics in computer science, where Stone duality provides a rigorous bridge. Formalizing this analogy could reveal new structural insights into the space of observables in quantum systems and the cohomological interpretation of L-functions.
proposals.hypothesis: The dual enveloping algebra (or Hopf algebra) structure of a symmetry, when viewed through a categorical lens of syntax-semantics duality, provides a natural and rigorous framework where its co-algebra operations (co-product, antipode) acquire direct physical meaning as operations on quantum observables or states.
proposals.method: We will construct a contravariant functor from a category of syntactic algebras (e.g., universal enveloping algebras of Lie algebras or quantum groups) to a category of semantic spaces (e.g., spaces of states or observables), inspired by Stone and Gelfand dualities. This will be implemented within the established framework of spectral triples and cyclic cohomology. The co-product will be interpreted via its action on characters (states), linking it to the moment problem and correlation functions, as explored in Connes' recent work on q-series and local factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified categorical foundation for interpreting dual Hopf algebra structures in physical contexts, potentially offering a new perspective on the spectral realization of zeta zeros. It aims to bridge techniques from noncommutative geometry, quantum groups, and categorical logic, creating a novel dictionary between algebraic operations and physical processes.
proposals.novelty: While dualities are well-studied, applying the specific categorical syntax-semantics analogy from computer science to assign concrete physical meaning to the co-algebraic structure of symmetries in noncommutative geometry is a novel synthesis. It directly connects Connes' spectral framework with the abstract theory of quantum groups and integrable systems.

proposals.title: A Riemann–Hilbert Correspondence for Zero-Dimensional QFT: Resurgence and Spectral Triples
proposals.motivation: The perturbative series of quantum field theories (QFTs) are typically divergent, requiring Borel resummation and the analysis of Stokes phenomena to capture non-perturbative effects. A rigorous, geometric framework for this resurgence structure is largely missing, especially one that connects discrete perturbative data to analytic functions. Zero-dimensional QFTs provide a perfect testing ground, analogous to the way zeta functions connect prime numbers to analytic properties.
proposals.hypothesis: The complete non-perturbative data (perturbative series, instanton weights, Stokes constants) of a zero-dimensional QFT can be encoded as the solution to a canonical Riemann–Hilbert problem, and this correspondence can be understood within the spectral triple framework of noncommutative geometry.
proposals.method: We will formulate a Riemann–Hilbert problem on the Borel plane for specific zero-dimensional integrals (e.g., quartic, Airy-type). The jump condition will be determined by the alien calculus of the formal series. The solution will yield the Borel-resummed partition function. We will then interpret the data (Stokes matrices, connection) as defining a 'resurgence spectral triple', where the Hilbert space is built from perturbative and non-perturbative sectors, and the Dirac operator encodes the Riemann–Hilbert connection. This constructs a direct analogy to the role of the Frobenius in the classical Riemann–Hilbert correspondence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, category-theoretic framework for resurgence in the simplest QFTs, directly linking perturbation theory to global analytic objects. It will establish a new bridge between isomonodromic deformation theory and quantum physics, and lay the algebraic-geometric foundation for applying these techniques to spectral triples arising from number theory, such as those in the 'zeta spectral triple' program.
proposals.novelty: While the Riemann–Hilbert correspondence is classic and resurgence is actively studied, their explicit fusion to define a 'resurgence spectral triple' for QFT is novel. Applying this noncommutative geometric lens to zero-dimensional path integrals creates a new dictionary between arithmetic geometry and quantum field theory.

proposals.title: Spectral Zeta Functions and the Riemann–Hilbert Correspondence for Zero-Dimensional Quantum Field Theories
proposals.motivation: The Riemann–Hilbert correspondence, a cornerstone of Connes's noncommutative geometry, establishes a deep link between flat vector bundles and monodromy representations, with profound implications for the analytic theory of differential equations. Zero-dimensional QFTs, while formally simple, encode rich combinatorial and algebraic structures through their partition functions and correlation functions, often as generating series. Applying the full Riemann–Hilbert framework to these theories offers a novel spectral perspective on their enumerative geometry, potentially revealing new connections to arithmetic.
proposals.hypothesis: The partition function and correlation functions of a zero-dimensional QFT can be realized as spectral zeta functions of a naturally associated spectral triple, and the Riemann–Hilbert correspondence for this triple will classify the asymptotic expansions of these functions in terms of monodromy data around singular points in the space of coupling constants.
proposals.method: We will construct explicit spectral triples for archetypal zero-dimensional QFTs (e.g., quartic, matrix models) by identifying the algebra of observables with a suitable function ring and defining a Dirac operator whose spectrum encodes the moments of the partition function. The associated spectral zeta function will be computed and analytically continued. The core technical step is to formulate and solve the Riemann–Hilbert problem for the regularized determinant of this Dirac operator, viewed as a function of complexified couplings, using methods from Connes's work on spectral triples and zeta regularization. This will involve analyzing the Stokes phenomena and monodromy representation associated with the asymptotic series.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous noncommutative geometric foundation for the asymptotic analysis of zero-dimensional QFTs, framing resurgent trans-series and instanton effects within the Riemann–Hilbert paradigm. It will yield a new class of computable examples of spectral triples where the zeta function has direct physical interpretation, potentially offering a toy model for understanding the spectral origin of the Riemann zeta zeros in a QFT context.
proposals.novelty: While zero-dimensional QFTs are well-studied, their systematic description via the complete Riemann–Hilbert apparatus of noncommutative geometry—linking their divergent expansions to monodromy in a spectral framework—is entirely novel and non-obvious from existing perturbative or resurgent analyses.

proposals.title: Spectral Hopf Algebras for Non-Lagrangian Quantum Field Theories
proposals.motivation: The Connes-Kreimer Hopf algebra of Feynman diagrams provides a profound algebraic-combinatorial framework for renormalization in perturbative Lagrangian QFT. However, many important quantum field theories, such as certain superconformal theories in higher dimensions or theories defined via their operator algebras, lack a known Lagrangian description. This gap prevents the application of this powerful algebraic renormalization machinery to a significant portion of the theoretical landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: A Hopf algebra governing the renormalization of non-Lagrangian theories can be constructed from the spectral data of their associated operator algebras, generalizing the role of Feynman graphs to more abstract combinatorial or spectral objects.
proposals.method: We will employ the framework of spectral triples and zeta functions, as developed in Connes's recent work, to define the primitive elements of the Hopf algebra. The method involves: 1) Identifying the appropriate 'spectral graph' or combinatorial object from the operator product expansion data or the spectrum of a relevant Dirac operator. 2) Using the zeta regularization of spectral triples to define the analog of Feynman integrals and their subdivergences. 3) Applying the Birkhoff decomposition on the associated group of characters, valued in the ring of meromorphic functions provided by the spectral zeta functions, to define the coproduct and renormalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the Connes-Kreimer formalism to a broader class of quantum field theories, providing a universal algebraic language for renormalization beyond perturbation theory around a classical action. It will forge a new link between noncommutative geometry, specifically the spectral theory of noncommutative spaces, and the structural problems of quantum field theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in replacing the Lagrangian and its associated Feynman graph calculus with intrinsic spectral data from the theory's operator algebra as the foundational input for the Hopf algebraic structure, a shift not previously attempted.

ideas.title: Derive Standard Model Feynman rules from the spectral action via cyclic cocycle calculus
ideas.description: This research program establishes a rigorous, computationally grounded bridge between Noncommutative Geometry (NCG) and Quantum Field Theory (QFT) by deriving perturbative expansion rules from the spectral action principle. Starting with the Connes-Chamseddine spectral triple for the Standard Model, it systematically computes the cyclic cocycles that arise from its heat kernel expansion and links them to specific diagrammatic rules for propagators and vertices. The core adversarial safeguard is to validate every step of the proposed dictionary by requiring it to reproduce known physical amplitudes in a chosen gauge, thereby ensuring the correspondence is not merely formal but physically consequential. The outcome will be a precise, testable schema showing how NCG's algebraic data generates the graphical calculus of particle interactions.

ideas.title: Dual Observables in Quantum Systems via Category Theory
ideas.description: This research expands the exploration of the dual enveloping algebra structure by drawing an analogy from category theory in computer science. The analogous problem is interpreting the relationship between program syntax and semantics via dualities like Stone duality. The solution constructs functorial mappings where syntactic operations correspond to semantic co-operations. Adapted to the original idea, the enveloping algebra (e.g., of a Lie algebra representing physical symmetries) is viewed as the 'syntax', while its dual is viewed as the space of 'physical observables' or states. The dual Hopf algebra structure—specifically its co-product, co-unit, and antipode—is then interpreted as describing how observables decompose, combine, and transform under the action of the symmetry. The research will formally develop this categorical duality to assign concrete physical meaning (e.g., to co-multiplication as a splitting of an observable into correlated parts under symmetry) to the abstract algebraic structure, potentially illuminating aspects of quantum groups, integrable systems, or non-commutative geometry in physics.

ideas.title: Classification and Harmonic Analysis on Semi-direct Products of Character Groups with Automorphism Groups
ideas.description: This research provides a complete structural and representation-theoretic analysis for a well-defined class of Lie groups: semi-direct products of the form G = Ĥ ⋊ Γ, where Ĥ is the Pontryagin dual of a fixed locally compact abelian group H, and Γ is a group of automorphisms acting on Ĥ. The work systematically classifies all admissible automorphism actions and the resulting group structures. The core deliverable is a full description of the unitary dual of G using Mackey theory, including explicit orbit-stabilizer calculations for the dual action of Γ on Ĥ, leading to a decomposition of the regular representation and a formula for the Plancherel measure.

ideas.title: Applying the Riemann–Hilbert Correspondence to Zero-Dimensional QFT via Analytic Number Theory
ideas.description: The core idea is to apply the full Riemann–Hilbert framework, typically used in the study of meromorphic connections and isomonodromic deformations, to the simplest possible setting of zero-dimensional quantum field theories (i.e., integrals over a finite number of variables). Drawing an analogy from analytic number theory, where discrete arithmetic data is connected to analytic properties of complex functions, we adapt that solution. In zero-dimensional QFT, the perturbative expansion in coupling constant is a formal, often divergent, power series (analogous to a sum over primes). The analogous solution is to use the Riemann–Hilbert correspondence to construct a direct link between this discrete perturbative data and the analytic structure of the Borel-transformed series. Specifically, we adapt the method by formulating a Riemann–Hilbert problem whose solution is the Borel-resummed partition function or correlator, with Stokes matrices encoding the non-perturbative instanton effects. This provides a rigorous, geometric framework to study resurgence and the complete non-perturbative structure in these toy models, serving as a foundational step for higher-dimensional generalizations.

ideas.description: This research direction constructs a rigorous, explicit dictionary between the perturbative world of zero-dimensional QFT and the algebraic geometry of cycles by leveraging the Riemann–Hilbert framework. In the simplified, finite-dimensional setting of zero-dimensional field theories, Feynman graphs correspond to concrete combinatorial sums, while the associated Riemann–Hilbert problem translates them into specific algebraic data (e.g., Stokes data, monodromy) tied to cyclic cocycles or algebraic cycles. The synergy enables a step-by-step categorial translation, providing a fully worked prototype that tests and refines the general QFT/NCG correspondence, ultimately aiming to export the dictionary to higher-dimensional theories.

ideas.title: Physical Signatures of Dual Hopf Structures in Quantum Integrable Models
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates the dual Hopf algebra structures (enveloping algebra and its dual) present in quantum integrable models, such as the XXZ spin chain or Lieb-Liniger gas, where they encode the algebra of conserved charges and the non-local statistics of quasiparticles. The core objective is to derive and propose unambiguous experimental signatures—such as distinctive features in the dynamic structure factor, characteristic finite-size corrections to entanglement entropy, or interferometric patterns in cold-atom simulations—that directly stem from this algebraic duality. By tethering the abstract mathematical exploration to these concrete physical observables, the work ensures the results are not only mathematically rigorous but also falsifiable and relevant to experimental quantum many-body physics.

ideas.title: Complete Analysis of the Lie Group G via Symmetry-Breaking in Crystallography
ideas.description: This research proposes a complete analysis of the semi-direct product structure of the Lie group G of characters by drawing an analogy from crystallography. In crystallography, complex structures are decomposed into a semi-direct product of a translation group (normal subgroup) and a point group, and their properties are fully determined by analyzing how the point group acts on the translational lattice. Adapting this solution, we treat G = N ⋊ H, where N is a normal Lie subgroup and H is a complementary Lie subgroup. The analysis will proceed by: first, fully characterizing the Lie group structure of N; second, classifying all possible smooth actions (Lie group homomorphisms H → Aut(N)) that define the semi-direct product, akin to classifying all symmetry-compatible distortions in a crystal; and third, synthesizing this to compute the complete structure of G—including its dimension, connected components, Lie algebra (as a semi-direct sum), representation theory, and topological properties. This method provides a systematic, decomposition-based framework to replace the placeholder 'FUTURE_WORK' with a concrete analytical plan.

ideas.title: Analyzing the Non-Abelian Diffeomorphism Group via Symmetry-Breaking Patterns in Condensed Matter
ideas.description: This research seed expands the analysis of the non-abelian group structure tied to diffeomorphisms by drawing an analogy from condensed matter physics. There, the solution to understanding complex material phases involves analyzing how a high-symmetry parent group is broken into subgroups, revealed by an order parameter and its associated topological defects. Adapted to this mathematical context, we propose to treat the full diffeomorphism group of a manifold as the parent symmetry. By introducing and varying geometric constraints (analogous to order parameters, such as specific tensor fields), we will systematically study the pattern of symmetry breaking. This involves classifying the resulting non-abelian subgroups of structure-preserving diffeomorphisms, analyzing their algebraic structure (e.g., commutator subgroups, central extensions), and exploring the geometric 'defects' or singularities that arise when such symmetries are only locally preserved. The outcome will be a structured framework for understanding the hierarchy and interplay of diffeomorphism groups in geometric topology and theoretical physics.

ideas.title: Riemann–Hilbert Reconstruction of Resurgent Trans-Series in Zero-Dimensional QFT Analogues
ideas.description: This research selects zero-dimensional field theory integrals that serve as rigorous analogues for higher-dimensional theories exhibiting resurgent asymptotics, such as those related to matrix models or topological strings. The core object of study is not the integral but the formal power series and its associated differential equation, derived via loop equations or other methods, which captures perturbative and non-perturbative sectors. The full Riemann–Hilbert framework is applied to this equation to explicitly reconstruct the resurgent trans-series, its Stokes constants, and the non-perturbative completion from its discontinuity data. This provides a concrete, solvable testbed for the Riemann–Hilbert methodology, validating its utility for decoding non-perturbative structure in QFT before application in physically dimensionful settings.

ideas.title: Non-Perturbative Lattice Simulation of Semi-Direct Product Gauge Groups via Hopf Algebra of Characters
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes the numerical implementation of Hopf algebras in lattice gauge theory with the complete structural analysis of the character group G. The core innovation is to use the explicit semi-direct product structure of G—analyzed from Idea B—to define the exact coproduct, antipode, and counit for its Hopf algebra, and then code these operations directly into a lattice simulation framework from Idea A. This allows for the non-perturbative study of gauge theories where the gauge group is the dual of G, a class previously inaccessible to systematic lattice investigation due to their non-trivial product structure. The result is a novel computational pathway to explore the phase structure and confinement properties of these theoretically rich but analytically opaque gauge theories.

ideas.title: Axiomatic Bootstrap for Perturbative Algebraic Structures in Non-Lagrangian QFT
ideas.description: This research program first establishes a minimal set of axioms for a perturbative quantum system, abstracting core concepts like unitarity, locality, and singularity structure without assuming a Lagrangian. From these axioms, it constructs a universal combinatorial Hopf algebra that encodes the decomposition of general processes into fundamental ones. The framework is then applied as a classification tool to concrete, suspected non-Lagrangian theories defined through other means (e.g., by their symmetry algebras or bootstrap equations), seeking to represent their perturbative expansions within this algebraic structure. Success would provide a rigorous, Lagrangian-independent definition of the Hopf algebra for those theories, while failure would pinpoint the essential physical features missing from the axiomatic foundation.

ideas.title: Adapting the Rosetta Stone: A Dictionary Between QFT Graphs and NCG Cycles via Category-Theoretic Functors
ideas.description: The core challenge in creating a dictionary between Feynman graphs in Quantum Field Theory (QFT) and cyclic cocycles in Noncommutative Geometry (NCG) is their fundamentally different syntactic structures—one combinatorial and the other algebraic/cohomological. Drawing an analogy from machine translation in computational linguistics, where languages with divergent grammars are translated via an intermediate semantic representation, this research idea proposes a category-theoretic solution. We adapt the 'interlingua' approach by defining an intermediate category (e.g., of 'perturbative invariants' or 'topological quantum data'). The analogous solution—using an abstract representation to bridge syntactic gaps—is adapted by constructing two explicit functors: F_QFT from a category of decorated Feynman graphs (with physical data like couplings) and F_NCG from a category of spectral triples with associated cyclic cocycles. Both functors target the intermediate category. The systematic dictionary is then the correspondence induced by these functors: a graph G and a cocycle φ are 'translations' if F_QFT(G) is isomorphic to F_NCG(φ) in the intermediate category. This approach automates and categorifies the translation, ensuring structural properties like composition of graphs/correlators are preserved, moving beyond a mere glossary to a functorial Rosetta Stone.

ideas.title: Non-Perturbative Riemann-Hilbert via Statistical Mechanics Lattice Methods
ideas.description: This idea explores non-perturbative formulations of the Riemann–Hilbert renormalization problem by drawing an analogy to the solution of non-perturbative problems in statistical mechanics. The analogous field is Statistical Mechanics and Condensed Matter Physics, where the challenge of solving models like the Ising model beyond perturbative expansions was met by developing lattice-based methods such as Monte Carlo simulations, tensor network renormalization (TRG), and exact lattice recursion relations. These techniques discretize the system's space and apply direct, non-perturbative computational or algebraic procedures. Adapted to the Riemann-Hilbert context, the research idea proposes to discretize the mathematical structure of the problem—potentially the contour, the sheaf of local solutions, or the underlying manifold—creating a 'lattice' or combinatorial framework. On this discrete structure, one would then implement adapted versions of non-perturbative renormalization group techniques (inspired by TRG or exact lattice methods) to solve the Riemann-Hilbert problem and compute renormalization group flows without relying on a perturbative expansion assumption. This approach seeks to access the strong-coupling regime and uncover global structures in the renormalization group that are inaccessible to conventional perturbative methods.

ideas.title: A Combinatorial Functor from Knots to Graphs with Birkhoff-Decomposed Invariant Filtrations
ideas.description: This research constructs an explicit combinatorial functor mapping a knot diagram to its chord intersection graph, providing a concrete input for the Connes-Kreimer Hopf algebra of graphs. The core of the study is to apply the algebraic Birkhoff decomposition to this graph, using a target algebra character derived from a classical knot invariant (e.g., the Jones polynomial). The resulting decomposition is shown to produce a novel, algebraically-defined filtration on the space of knots, which is then rigorously compared to known topological complexity measures. The system's robustness is tested by verifying if this filtration can detect non-invertibility, estimate unknotting numbers, or provide a new algebraic proof of the existence of Vassiliev invariants, thereby ensuring the theory yields concrete, computable knot-theoretic results.

ideas.title: Interpreting Anomalous Dimensions as Fractal Scaling in Renormalization Group Flow
ideas.description: This research seed proposes interpreting anomalous dimensions in quantum field theory by drawing an analogy to the measurement and interpretation of fractal dimensions in critical phenomena. In statistical physics, systems at a critical point exhibit scale invariance, and their effective dimensionality becomes fractional, characterized by critical exponents obtained through finite-size scaling. The analogous solution is to adapt the finite-size scaling methodology to QFT's renormalization group framework. The adapted idea treats the RG scale parameter analogously to the system size in statistical mechanics. By analyzing how physical observables depend on this scale, one can extract the anomalous dimension not merely as a technical factor but as a direct indicator of the scale-dependent, quasi-fractal geometry of the field operator's 'size' or influence within the theory's space of couplings. This provides a geometric and intuitive interpretation of anomalous dimensions as measuring how an operator's effective dimensionality changes with the energy scale, enriching the mathematical structure of RG flow with a conceptual framework from fractal geometry.

ideas.title: Adapting Renormalization Group Flow from Condensed Matter to Lattice Gauge Theory
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from condensed matter physics—specifically, the numerical implementation of renormalization group flows via methods like DMRG and Tensor Network Renormalization—to the problem of scaling up lattice gauge theory calculations to non-perturbative regimes. The analogous problem in condensed matter involved accessing strong-correlation quantum critical points beyond perturbation theory by developing numerical schemes that preserve essential physics (like entanglement) during coarse-graining. The adapted idea is to implement the Hopf algebraic structures of a lattice gauge theory numerically within a similar real-space RG framework. The core innovation is to design a coarse-graining procedure for the gauge lattice that respects and propagates the Hopf-algebraic constraints (e.g., gauge invariance, coproduct rules) at each step. This would enable a controlled numerical flow from the lattice scale toward the continuum, allowing the study of non-perturbative fixed points, phase transitions, and the approach to the continuum limit in a way that inherently moves beyond perturbation theory.

ideas.title: Axiomatic characterization of Birkhoff-compatible regularization schemes
ideas.description: This research program aims to move beyond the specific technique of dimensional regularization by establishing the foundational principles that make the Birkhoff decomposition effective. It will first formalize the minimal set of algebraic (e.g., Rota-Baxter, Hopf algebra compatibility) and analytic (e.g., singularity structure) axioms required for a regularization scheme to admit a clean Birkhoff decomposition. The core work then involves surveying known schemes against this axiomatic benchmark and investigating the construction of new regularization methods—possibly with different physical interpretations like a fundamental momentum scale or geometric cutoff—that satisfy these axioms. The ultimate goal is to determine if the reliance on dim reg is a historical contingency or a mathematical necessity, thereby either justifying its unique role or discovering viable, conceptually distinct alternatives.

ideas.title: Constructive Resurgent Riemann–Hilbert Correspondence for Singularly Perturbed Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a constructive bridge between resurgent analysis and Riemann–Hilbert problems by focusing on a concrete class of singularly perturbed meromorphic connections. It defines a 'resurgent Riemann–Hilbert map' that sends a formal transseries solution to its associated Stokes data, interpreted as a resurgent symplectomorphism acting on the Borel plane. The core innovation is an algorithmic procedure that solves the factorization problem iteratively on successive Riemann sheets of the Borel transform, thereby explicitly computing the alien derivatives governing the Stokes phenomenon. This yields a computable framework to derive the complete transseries structure, including non-perturbative sectors, from the original differential system.

ideas.title: Inverse Spectral Problem: From Group Character to Graph Laplacian Spectrum
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy to inverse spectral problems in graph theory and physics. The analogous problem is reconstructing a graph from the spectrum of its Laplacian matrix, which is solved using combinatorial constraints and iterative spectral matching algorithms. Adapted to the original context, the idea is to interpret the given character of group G as defining a target operator spectrum (e.g., of a graph-derived operator related to the Feynman diagram's structure). The research would develop a reconstruction algorithm that uses this 'group character spectrum' to constrain and search the space of possible Feynman graphs (including topology, edge weights, or interaction types). The SCALING_DUAL dimension informs how the asymptotic scaling behavior of the character relates to the graph's complexity parameters, guiding the inverse construction from group representation data back to the underlying graph configuration.

ideas.title: Birkhoff-Decomposition-Based Renormalization for Stochastic PDEs Beyond Dimensional Regularization
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes the algebraic renormalization method of Birkhoff decomposition—originally tied to dimensional regularization in perturbative QFT—with the problem of regularization in stochastic PDEs (SPDEs). The core innovation is to generalize the Birkhoff decomposition approach to work with regularization schemes suitable for SPDEs on curved domains or with non-scale-invariant noise, such as lattice cut-off, heat kernel regularization, or wavelet-based mollification. By doing so, it provides a universal algebraic procedure to separate divergent counterterms from finite, physical expectations for observables in SPDEs, even in settings lacking a straightforward dimensional analytic continuation. This leads to a mathematically robust renormalization theory for singular SPDEs like the dynamical Φ⁴₃ model on Riemannian manifolds, independent of any specific regularization choice.

ideas.title: Adapt Phylogenetic Invariant Methods from Computational Biology to Hopf Ideal Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational biology, specifically the use of computational algebraic geometry to find phylogenetic invariants that characterize evolutionary tree models. In that field, Gröbner basis techniques are used to compute a finite generating set for the ideal of polynomials that vanish on the algebraic variety of probability distributions for a given tree. We transfer this methodology to the study of Hopf algebras. Here, the 'model' is the Hopf algebra itself, defined by its structure maps. By encoding these maps (product, coproduct, antipode, etc.) as polynomials in a suitably large ring, we can define a 'Hopf ideal' whose vanishing locus corresponds to the algebra satisfying all Hopf axioms. Computing a Gröbner basis for this ideal yields a complete set of algebraic constraints (analogous to phylogenetic invariants) that characterize the Hopf structure. This computational approach can then be applied to analyze properties like isomorphism, study deformations via perturbation of the ideal's generators, and test whether a given algebra or coalgebra belongs to a specific Hopf class by checking ideal membership.

ideas.title: Computational Hopf-Ideal Regularization for Singular Stochastic PDEs
ideas.description: This research direction applies computational algebraic geometry, specifically Groebner basis techniques for Hopf ideal analysis, to the problem of extracting finite values in singular stochastic PDEs. It formalizes the renormalization procedure by treating the Hopf algebra of Feynman diagrams as a computational algebraic object, where the ideals defining the subtraction scheme can be algorithmically manipulated. This allows for the systematic generation and comparison of finite-dimensional approximations (regularizations) that respect the underlying Hopf-algebraic structure, such as the coproduct and counit. Consequently, one can derive new, algebraically-guaranteed regularization schemes for SPDEs and computationally verify their consistency and stability properties.

ideas.title: Apply the Wiener-Hopf Factorization from Signal Processing to the Riemann–Hilbert/Resurgence Framework
ideas.description: The original idea connects Riemann–Hilbert factorization to resurgent analysis. By analogy, the Wiener-Hopf factorization from signal processing solves a similar problem of reconstructing a function from its spectral data by enforcing analyticity in complementary regions. Adapting this, we treat the Riemann–Hilbert data for a resurgent function—the Stokes multipliers encoding jumps across singular rays—as the input. The solution method is to implement a factorization procedure that systematically decomposes the associated Riemann–Hilbert problem into components analytic in successive sectors of the Borel plane. This provides an algorithmic, constructive framework to derive the explicit transseries expansion and resurgent structure from the discontinuity data, effectively operationalizing the abstract Riemann–Hilbert correspondence for asymptotic series.

ideas.title: Adapting Renormalization Group Flow from Quantum Field Theory to Stochastic PDEs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the renormalization group (RG) flow procedure from Quantum Field Theory (QFT) to address regularization in stochastic PDEs. In QFT, RG flow extracts finite physical predictions from formally divergent integrals by integrating out high-energy modes and tracking how coupling constants flow with an energy scale cutoff. The analogous solution is to treat a stochastic PDE with high-frequency noise: the high-wavenumber components of the stochastic forcing are analogous to the divergent high-energy modes. By systematically integrating out these high-wavenumber modes above a scale Λ, one can derive an effective, renormalized equation for the large-scale, low-frequency dynamics. The adapted research idea is to develop a rigorous mathematical framework for this RG flow in the context of stochastic PDEs (e.g., stochastic Navier-Stokes or reaction-diffusion equations), defining how the effective noise covariance and other parameters flow as the cutoff Λ is lowered, thereby extracting finite, meaningful statistical properties for the observable solutions.

ideas.title: Structure-Aware Renormalization for Singular Stochastic PDEs via Commuting Limits
ideas.description: This research develops a mathematically rigorous framework for applying renormalization (extraction of finite values) to regularization in stochastic PDEs. It overcomes the fatal flaw of a generic approach by tailoring the extraction procedure to the specific regularity structure or paracontrolled formulation of a target singular SPDE, such as the dynamical Φ⁴₃ model. The central technical aim is to design the renormalization scheme so that the operation of taking the probabilistic limit (e.g., in probability) commutes with the extraction of finite constants from divergent terms. This ensures the final solution object is both well-defined and physically meaningful within its intended analytical and probabilistic setting.

ideas.title: Operational Protocol for Measuring Anomalous Dimensions
ideas.description: This research designs a specific operational procedure—a measurement or simulation protocol—whose outcome is directly governed by the value of an anomalous dimension. Instead of a mathematical interpretation, the idea provides a causal link: executing the protocol on a system at one scale predicts a quantifiable result at another scale, with the anomalous dimension as the essential scaling factor. The approach is validated by applying the same protocol to diverse systems (e.g., different critical models) and showing the anomalous dimension correctly predicts the scaling collapse of the data. This transforms the anomalous dimension from a formal exponent into an empirically accessible quantity defined by a repeatable physical operation.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Graph Reconstruction from Multi-Scale Representation Data
ideas.description: This approach addresses the ill-posedness of the original inverse problem by shifting from deterministic reconstruction to probabilistic inference. Instead of a single character, it uses the complete branching data—how the representation decomposes under a filtration of subgroups—to create a highly informative fingerprint of the underlying graph. A machine learning model, specifically a graph neural network within a variational inference framework, is trained to map this multi-scale representation data to a distribution over possible Feynman graphs. The system outputs not a single graph but a ranked set of plausible candidates with associated confidence measures, robustly handling the inherent ambiguity.
